Dr. @ShahMeenesh , Chairman, NDDB & Mother Dairy alongwith Shri JT Chary, MD, Mother Dairy unveiled India's first naturally degradable milk pouch in soil developed by NDDB's wholly-owned subsidiary @MotherDairyMilk in New Delhi. On the occasion, Dr. Shah stated that as the world’s largest milk producer, the Indian dairy sector reflects scale, inclusivity and responsibility, with sustainability intrinsically embedded across the dairy value chain. He mentioned that Mother Dairy's innovation is India’s first naturally degradable milk pouch in soil — a first-of-its-kind packaging breakthrough that transforms into bioavailable wax and is naturally broken down by microbes in soil into natural elements. While remaining recyclable, the pouch naturally degrades in soil within a few years, thereby taking an additional step towards addressing the challenge of fugitive plastic. 

Chairman, NDDB highlighted that milk pouch packaging remains crucial for ensuring affordable and convenient access to milk at scale in India and that the innovation was developed after over four years of dedicated research without requiring any change in consumer usage behaviour. He reiterated that the initiative would not impact consumer milk prices. He further stated that Mother Dairy has been among the early adopters of Extended Producer Responsibility (EPR) and has established itself as a Plastic Waste Neutral organisation, reaffirming NDDB and Mother Dairy’s commitment towards responsible innovation, accessibility, affordability and long-term sustainability.

The new packaging innovation will be introduced through Mother Dairy's Cow Milk variant across Delhi NCR from World Environment Day, 2026.

Is INDIA ready for the PV waste boom?
Are PV wastes a problem or a new opportunity?

As millions of solar panels are installed across India, another challenge is slowly emerging: what happens when these panels reach the end of their 25–30 year lifetimes?

According to the Council on Energy, Environment and Water (CEEW), cumulative waste from existing and new solar PV capacity could rise from 161 kilotonnes in 2025 to 594 kilotonnes by 2030, then surge to almost 5 million tonnes by 2040 and nearly 19 million tonnes by 2050. That is roughly a 30-fold increase between 2030 and 2050.

However, these end-of-life panels are not simply waste. Up to 95% of the materials inside PV modules can potentially be recovered, including glass, aluminum, copper, silicon and small amounts of critical minerals.

Recognizing this challenge, India added solar panels to its e-waste regulations in 2022 and introduced an Extended Producer Responsibility (EPR) framework. Manufacturers, producers and recyclers are required to register and participate in the system, while the country aims to achieve an 80% recycling rate for electronic waste by 2028–29.

At the same time, important gaps remain. There is little publicly available information on how much PV waste is actually being recycled, whether targets are being met, or how much material has been recovered.

In other words, India appears to view future PV waste not only as an environmental issue but also as a potential resource. If effective recycling systems are developed, today's solar panels could become tomorrow's "urban mines", supplying valuable materials and supporting a new domestic recycling industry
Source: @CEEWIndia @ZerocarbonA

One Portal. Every Waste Stream. Zero Compliance Chaos.

India just consolidated its EPR (Extended Producer Responsibility) ecosystem and producers now have every reason to log in and take a closer look.

CPCB (Central Pollution Control Board) has rolled out the Common EPR Portal, replacing the standalone Plastic Waste EPR Portal with a single, unified SSO (Single Sign-On) covering multiple EPR frameworks. Producers, Importers, and Brand Owners (PIBOs), recyclers, and other registered stakeholders now operate under one system instead of scattered, stream-specific portals.

Existing data is being migrated automatically but the responsibility to verify accuracy now rests with stakeholders, not CPCB.

Why this shift matters:

– One login, multiple waste streams no more duplicate registrations
– Centralized reporting builds real transparency and traceability
– Administrative overlap drops sharply across compliance teams
– Registrations, filings, and records finally live under one roof
– A stronger digital backbone for India's circular economy push

What stakeholders need to do right now:

– Log in through the new Common EPR Portal using SSO
– Review every migrated registration and compliance record line by line
– Report discrepancies immediately waiting until audit season is not an option

A unified system is only as reliable as the data feeding it. The portal is live, and the window to verify records is now. 

Could this be the infrastructure moment India's circular economy has been waiting for?

#EPR #CPCB #CircularEconomy #WasteManagement #PolymerIndustry

The ICSSR–CAG Colloquium held on 13 July 2026 at the R.A. Poddar Auditorium, Institute of Management, University of Rajasthan, Jaipur, on the theme "Using CAG Data for Social Science Research and Strengthening Solid Waste Management Governance," brought together academicians, researchers, students, public administrators, and audit professionals to deliberate on the role of authentic government data in evidence-based policymaking and sustainable urban governance. The programme underscored the importance of collaboration between the Indian Council of Social Science Research (ICSSR), the Comptroller and Auditor General (CAG), universities, and the research community in advancing social science research and informed public discourse.

The programme commenced with a welcome address by Shri Ramavatar Sharma, who welcomed the participants and emphasized the growing importance of authentic government data in governance and policymaking. He encouraged students to utilise government reports and datasets for research and highlighted the significance of CAG reports in strengthening public finance and accountability.

Prof. Alpana Kateja highlighted data as a powerful tool for governance and public policy, emphasizing accessibility, democratization, and informed interpretation. Ms. Mani K. introduced ICSSR's fellowships, grants, and research opportunities, encouraging scholars to undertake evidence-based and policy-oriented studies. Shri Ravindra Yadavexplained the constitutional mandate of the CAG and introduced key publications, including the Annual Accounts, State Finance Audit Reports, Public Finance Statistics, and Fiscal Health Indicators, demonstrating how these datasets support trend analysis, inter-state comparisons, and public policy research. Prof. Ram Singh emphasized stronger collaboration between academia and public institutions and encouraged interdisciplinary research using authentic public finance data to promote transparency, accountability, and sustainable development.

The discussions on public finance and governance examined public finance as an instrument of public welfare, fiscal discipline, revenue and expenditure management, fiscal deficits, debt sustainability, financial accountability, and evidence-based policymaking. Deliberations on urban governance and municipal finance highlighted the need to strengthen the financial capacity of Urban Local Bodies, municipal finance reforms, and improved urban service delivery while addressing challenges relating to waste management, water quality, air pollution, public health, and urban infrastructure.

A dedicated session on Solid Waste Management (SWM) examined India's evolving waste management framework and audit findings, focusing on source segregation, scientific collection, treatment, recycling and disposal, decentralized composting, biomethanation, Material Recovery Facilities (MRFs), and the management of e-waste and legacy waste. The discussions also examined the Proposed Solid Waste Management Rules, 2026, highlighting reforms such as the Polluter Pays Principle, digital waste tracking, mandatory Refuse Derived Fuel (RDF), stricter landfill regulations, scientific remediation of legacy waste, and global best practices including Extended Producer Responsibility (EPR) and circular economy approaches.

The programme concluded with recommendations to strengthen solid waste management through universal source segregation, scientific waste processing, digitization of waste management systems, stronger monitoring and accountability, enhanced citizen participation, public awareness, and the promotion of the principles of Reduce, Reuse, and Recycle (3Rs). The discussions reaffirmed that authentic government data and public audit findings are invaluable resources for strengthening social science research, evidence-based policymaking, and accountable governance.

India's recycling industry is moving from a niche business to a strategic growth sector.

With rising e-waste, battery usage, plastic consumption and stricter environmental regulations, recycling is becoming an important pillar of India's circular economy.

Government incentives, EPR (Extended Producer Responsibility) rules, critical mineral recovery and sustainability goals are creating long-term opportunities for organized recycling companies.

📰 Latest Sector Update

The Government has approved a 1,500-crore incentive scheme to promote recycling of critical minerals from e-waste and end-of-life products, aiming to strengthen domestic supply chains & reduce import dependence. 
This is expected to benefit the organized recycling ecosystem over the coming years.

📈 Why this theme matters

♻️ India is rapidly adopting a circular economy.
🔋 EV battery recycling demand is accelerating.
💻 E-waste generation continues to grow every year.
🏭 Industrial metal recycling is becoming increasingly important.
🌍 ESG investing is driving capital toward sustainable businesses.
🧲 Critical mineral recovery has become a strategic priority.
🚛 Organized waste management is replacing the unorganized sector.
🏛️ Government policies continue to support recycling infrastructure.

👀 Key Triggers to Watch

✅ Critical mineral recycling incentives.
✅ EPR policy implementation.
✅ Battery recycling expansion.
✅ Aluminium & lead recycling demand.
✅ E-waste collection growth.
✅ Capacity expansion.
✅ Commodity price trends.
✅ Export opportunities.
✅ New recycling technologies.
✅ Circular economy initiatives.

🔍 Recent Company Updates

🔹 Gravita India – Continues expanding its lead, aluminium and plastic recycling operations across multiple countries while increasing capacity in value-added recycled products.

🔹 Eco Recycling (Ecoreco) – One of India's pioneers in e-waste recycling, benefiting from tighter EPR compliance and rising demand for responsible electronic waste management.

🔹 Antony Waste Handling Cell – Continues to benefit from increasing municipal solid waste management projects as Indian cities expand organized waste processing infrastructure.

🔹 Ganesha Ecosphere – Remains India's leading PET bottle recycler, supported by growing demand for recycled polyester from global apparel and textile brands.

🔹 Baheti Recycling Industries – Expanding its aluminium recycling business to capitalize on increasing demand for recycled non-ferrous metals.

🔹 CMR Green Technologies – Recently made a strong stock market debut, underscoring investor interest in India's aluminium recycling sector.

🔹 VA Tech Wabag – Continues winning industrial and municipal water treatment projects, supporting long-term wastewater recycling growth.

🔹 Ion Exchange (India) – Expanding water treatment and industrial water recycling solutions across domestic and international markets.

India's recycling industry is no longer just about waste management it's becoming a strategic sector linked to resource security, sustainability, critical minerals & green manufacturing. 
Companies with strong recycling capabilities could benefit as the circular economy continues to expand.

quotedTweet.text:
In Depth Analysis of Recycling Theme ♻️ A thread 🧵 👉

EPR, which stands for Extended Producer Responsibility, is a fundamental requirement for Producers, Importers, and Brand Owners (PIBOs) involved in the generation of Plastic, Battery, or e-waste in India.

❇️How effective is EPR?

EPR policies have been known to contribute to packaging waste reduction and increase recycling activities. EPR policies are having a positive impact throughout the years in many countries.

❇️How EPR Works?

The liability of extended producer responsibility gets passed on from the seller of a commodity to its
purchaser – in this case, plastic packaging –to avoid duplication in accounting. It means that when a producer buys plastic from an importer, and then directly places the product on the market, the liability to collect back and recycle falls on the producer.

Similarly, if a brand owner buys plastic from a producer or an importer and uses it to place its products on the market, the brand owner becomes liable for end-of-life management of that amount of plastic waste.

The collection target (also known as EPR target) is arrived at by calculating the average of the amount of the plastic that a polluter has placed on the market in the last two consecutive years. 

EPR is ‘brand neutral’ – this
means that a polluter does not necessarily limit itself to its own plastic packaging while collecting back, it can collect and recycle plastics put on the market by any other brand to fulfil its EPR target

❇️Note-The EPR certificates can only be issued to the ‘polluters’ by authorised plastic waste processors/recyclers.

If you like the thread, Please bookmark and retweet 🔁

#EPR #RecyclingTheme

EPR India.text:
Reasons why EPR is the solution to Plastic Recycling?

🌟Environmental Protection
▫️EPR enables plastic recycling, which is the core principle of the policy tool.

👉Reduce overall packaging use
👉Improve collection and recycling of packaging waste
👉Improve packaging designs for greater recyclability and reusability
👉Increase resource efficiency by reducing the use of virgin material
Halt plastic leakages into nature

🌟Social Benefits

👉Job creation along the packaging value chain
👉Integrate informal sector, improve health and safety of waste pickers and collectors
👉Formalisation of informal recycling practices

#epr #environment #theme

🌟Why EPR Policies are Required?

👉EPR policies are being developed and implemented globally to address the urgent issue of plastic pollution. These policies are designed to improve the efficiency and transparency of data management, financing, collection, and recycling of plastics.

👉The United Nations Environment Programme has partnered with various organizations to increase capacity to develop and implement EPR approaches for plastic products, aiming to harmonize these approaches globally

👉 The concept is gaining support from global companies and organizations as a mechanism to provide dedicated funding to increase recycling rates and reduce plastic pollution. Phasing out single-use and short-lived plastic products by the largest polluters could greatly reduce global plastic pollution.

#epr #india

🚨6 lakh fake pollution-trading certificates unearthed in three State.

👉The malpractice came to light after the CPCB conducted physical checks on their

👉These certificates, or EPR certificates, are used by thousands of companies that use plastic packaging in some form for business operations and are legally obliged to either ensure that a prescribed percentage of the plastic used annually is collected and recycled, or that they buy enough certificates to make good on their targets.

👉The potential number of fake certificates could be manifold as these were only four of the 2,348 plastic waste recyclers registered with the

👉The Central Pollution Control Board (CPCB)  had unearthed more than 6,00,000 fake pollution-trading certificates from audits at four plastic-recycling companies in Gujarat, Maharashtra and Karnataka, public documents show.

👉The MPCB seized nearly 300 kg of plastic below 300 microns in size from Kondhwa-based ‘Jalaram Plastic’ The unit has not obtained an extended producer responsibility (EPR) certificate from the competent authority, The board on July 10 issued closure notice to the manufacturing unit in question.

#epr #india

🌟The Future of EPR Consulting in India

💡As India continues to grow economically, the role of EPR consultants will only become more critical. The government is tightening environmental regulations, and consumers are demanding more responsible products and practices. EPR consultants are at the forefront of this shift, helping businesses adapt and thrive in a greener economy.

💡Conclusion

👉The EPR Consultant in India is a key player in the transition towards sustainable business practices. 👉The journey towards sustainability is complex, but with the right guidance, businesses can unlock their true potential and contribute positively to the global environmental landscape.

🌟India’s EPR framework targets specific waste streams including Plastic Packaging Waste, eWaste, Tyre Waste, Battery Waste, and Waste Oil, with policies evolving to cover End of Life Vehicles (ELV). This approach promotes environmental sustainability and supports the infrastructure for recycling and recovery, marking a significant step towards a circular economy.

India is one of the worst places on earth for cleanliness and trash

- India is the world’s top plastic polluter
- Around 35% of India’s waste is properly treated, the rest dumped in open landfills and streets
- India is among the highest sources of plastic entering the oceans. Combined with countries like Indonesia, it contributes more ocean plastic than the next several nations, including the US, put together
- India has thousands of overflowing open landfills and dumpsites, Delhi’s Ghazipur landfill is over 200 feet high 
- The Ganges River alone carries around 315 tons of plastic waste daily, equivalent to the weight of about 79 elephants

These numbers are only increasing

Young volunteers from Swach Roots Foundation (@swachroots) have launched Swachroots, a community-driven cleanliness initiative to remove litter and plastic waste from public spaces. The volunteers carried out clean-up drives across urban walkways, riverbanks and nature trails, collecting waste with gloves and trash bags.

The volunteers gathered at heavily littered locations and removed plastic waste and other debris from sidewalks, pedestrian paths, riverbanks and canals. They also cleaned forested and hilly nature trails where waste had been left behind by visitors.

The video captures volunteers working together across multiple locations as part of the Swachroots initiative. By sharing their clean-up efforts online, the foundation aims to inspire more people to avoid littering and take part in keeping their surroundings clean.

"😢 Juhu Beach, Mumbai — once a symbol of Mumbai’s vibrant coastline, now drowning in plastic waste.  

Look at these heartbreaking scenes: mountains of single-use plastics, bottles, bags, wrappers, and microplastics choking the sand and sea. Waves that should bring life are now delivering more trash.  

**How plastics are destroying our oceans:**  
- Plastics never truly disappear — they break down into microplastics that enter the food chain.  
- Marine life mistakes plastic for food → turtles, fish, seabirds, and even sharks (as seen in recent reports) ingest it, leading to starvation, internal injuries, and death.  
- Toxins from plastics leach into water, contaminating seafood we eat.  
- Entire ecosystems suffocate under plastic sludge, destroying mangroves, coral, and breeding grounds.  

**What we do to nature returns to us.**  
Pollute the ocean and it returns as contaminated fish on our plates, flooded cities from disrupted climate, and toxic air. Harm nature and nature harms us back — this is not punishment, it’s simple cause and effect.

In Sikkim’s Lachen village, plastic waste is not allowed to enter. Vehicles are checked for single-use plastic bottles before heading towards Gurudongmar Lake — one of India’s highest glacial lakes.

This culture of sustainability began decades ago, with Sikkim becoming India’s first state to ban plastic bags in 1998.

Through discipline, community participation, and simple alternatives like bamboo bottles, Lachen shows that protecting nature is possible.

Meet the “Plastic Man of India” – Prof. Dr. R. Vasudevan from Madurai, Tamil Nadu!

He pioneered a brilliant way to recycle plastic waste into super-durable roads by shredding it and mixing with bitumen. 

Started with a small stretch on his campus in 2002 — it’s still standing strong today! These roads are tougher, waterproof, heat-resistant, and help clear lakhs of tonnes of plastic waste across India. 

Awarded the Padma Shri in 2018 for this green innovation.
Real change through science! 🇮🇳♻️

Sunfeast YiPPee! by ITC presents the “YiPPee! Better World: Create Magic” initiative in partnership with the Times of India. With this three-month initiative, the brand is set to convert post-consumption #plasticwaste to playground equipment for parks in Delhi-NCR, Mumbai, Bangalore, and Kolkata! If you are in one of these cities, you have the exciting opportunity to be a part of the initiative, and make a difference to local children’s lives!

To do this, you simply have to vote for the park of your choice. With multiple communication touch points on TV, print, social media, and radio; Sunfeast YiPPee! hopes to reach out to as many citizens in these cities as possible to drive action. You can also join a large-scale plastic collection drive hosted by Sunfeast YiPPee!.  Plastic waste donated by city dwellers, at designated collection centres, will be upcycled to make new swing sets, benches, see-saws etc. for the four parks that receive the most votes in each city.

Through “Create Magic”, Sunfeast YiPPee! wants to spread awareness and make a case for how post-consumption plastic waste can be utilized innovatively to delight people, in ways that are tangible and impact their everyday lives positively.

To join in the initiative and do your bit to Create Magic, visit https://t.co/beMdi5Wctp

India generates more than 50,000 tons of lithium-ion battery waste every year & is growing in the range of 40-80% depending on different models used for computing EV growth in India. But what's the plan for Attero Recycling? Know at: https://t.co/yeXpcdUdT1

India has quietly taken a strategic step in the critical minerals value chain by restricting the export of black mass — a material that remains largely unknown to mainstream investors but plays a crucial role in battery recycling.

In a single year, India reportedly exported black mass containing approximately 350 tonnes of cobalt, 71.7 tonnes of lithium, and 215 tonnes of nickel. These valuable minerals were not extracted from mines; they were recovered from discarded lithium-ion batteries. While India imports these critical minerals at significant cost, a large portion of their recovered value was being exported overseas.

Black mass is generated after shredding lithium-ion batteries and typically contains 40–50% of the battery’s weight in recoverable materials, including lithium, cobalt, nickel, and other strategic elements. Due to the advanced refining capabilities and economies of scale available in countries like China and South Korea, overseas buyers often offered better prices than domestic refiners. As a result, India retained the lower-value shredding activity while exporting the higher-value mineral recovery opportunity.

The recent policy changes indicate a clear shift in India’s approach — moving from simple battery waste processing toward domestic critical mineral refining.

Key policy developments:

🔹 January 2025: The MoEFCC Technical Review Committee classified black mass as hazardous waste unless refined at the same premises. Export restrictions were tightened, requiring government approval and limiting exports to authorised end users.

🔹 October 2025: The ₹1,500 crore Critical Mineral Recycling Scheme was introduced, offering a 20% capital subsidy. However, eligibility is focused on advanced chemical recyclers capable of achieving 80% recovery rates with 99% purity standards — effectively excluding businesses focused only on shredding and exporting.

🔹 April 2026: Under the scheme, 58 companies received approval, marking the beginning of a more structured domestic recycling ecosystem.

The message is clear: the policy does not aim to eliminate black mass production; it aims to discourage the low-value “shred and export” model and encourage value-added refining within India.

This creates a significant opportunity as India’s battery recycling sector remains at an early stage. Currently, only a small fraction of end-of-life lithium-ion batteries are recycled into usable materials, while NITI Aayog estimates that India could generate around 128 GWh of recyclable battery capacity by 2030.

Companies such as Gravita India, which has expanded into lithium-ion battery recycling, along with players like Tata Chemicals, Exide Industries, and Amara Raja Energy & Mobility, are positioned in different parts of the emerging ecosystem. Private players such as Lohum and Attero are also active in advanced recycling and material recovery.

India’s next opportunity may not just be in manufacturing batteries, but in controlling the entire battery lifecycle — collection, recycling, refining, and recovery of critical minerals.

Educational purpose only. This is not a buy/sell recommendation or investment advice.

*Ecoreco Leads India's Strategic Push for Recycling and Critical Mineral Management: A Path to Sustainable Development*

Recycling stands as a cornerstone of sustainable development, mirroring nature's own cycles of resource replenishment. Recognizing its vital role, the Indian government has intensified efforts to promote the recycling of critical resources, including e-waste, lithium-ion batteries, and end-of-life vehicles. Through stringent regulations under the Environment Protection Act and the establishment of authorized recycling facilities, India is addressing the need for effective waste management. Eco Recycling Ltd (Ecoreco), a trailblazer in this field, has been at the forefront since 2005, serving as the first authorized facility for e-waste recycling. Their efforts are aligned with the government’s Extended Producer Responsibility (EPR) framework, ensuring that producers meet their obligations by getting electronic and battery waste recycled, which eventually produces precious & rare earth metals.

In a recent budget speech, Finance Minister Nirmala Sitharaman highlighted the significance of recycling in securing critical minerals essential for advanced sectors such as nuclear energy, telecommunications, and defense. The proposed Critical Mineral Mission aims to bolster domestic production, recycling, and international acquisition of these vital resources. Ecoreco supports this initiative, emphasizing the importance of recycling e-waste and lithium-ion batteries to ensure a steady supply of precious and rare metals. With their expanded capacity to process 31,200 MTPA of E-waste and 6000 MTPA of Li-ion Batteries, Ecoreco plays a crucial role in supporting the government’s vision for sustainable mineral management and EPR compliance, furthering the nation’s path toward a circular economy.

India generates 70,000 metric tons of battery waste each year. When Anupam Kumar and Arvind Bharadwaj began working with Lithium-ion batteries, they were struck by how much valuable material was being discarded. Most of these batteries end up in landfills, harming the environment, while India remains dependent on importing critical minerals present in batteries like Lithium, Cobalt, and Nickel.

Determined to change this, Anupam and Arvind founded Mini Mines, which ACT For Environment is proud to support. Their mission: to recycle #waste batteries, recover precious materials, and reduce our reliance on imports—helping conserve natural resources and build a sustainable, self-reliant future for India. ♻️

Watch their inspiring journey in the video below as they work to power India’s transition to a cleaner tomorrow. https://t.co/EUQnO25ah2

#tgvsraac #TGV Caustic soda companies, get ready! as India's focus on battery recycling creates a massive opportunity 

💫India's Policy Push: 
The Ministry of Environment, Forest and Climate Change has strengthened the Battery Waste Management (Amendment) Rules, 2025. These rules reinforce the Extended Producer Responsibility (EPR) framework, mandating higher collection and recycling targets for producers, and require them to recover critical metals like lithium and cobalt. The new rules, including the requirement for QR codes and a centralized online portal, aim to enhance accountability and traceability.

💫Budgetary Support: 
The Union Budget 2025 exempted customs duties on key materials like lithium-ion battery scrap, cobalt, and zinc. This policy aims to reduce costs and make domestic battery recycling more economically viable.

💫Caustic Soda's Role: 
Caustic soda is a key chemical used in the hydrometallurgical process to extract and recover valuable metals, such as lithium and cobalt, from spent batteries.

💫Market Growth: 

The caustic soda market in India is already on a growth trajectory, with the market expected to grow from 2.7 Million Tons in 2024 to 4.0 Million Tons by 2033. This growth is driven by various industries including textiles, paper, and aluminum, and the new demand from battery recycling will further contribute to this.

💫Geopolitical Factors: 

Geopolitical tensions and export controls, such as China's policies on critical minerals, are pushing countries like India to strengthen their domestic supply chains and reduce reliance on imports. This creates a strategic imperative for India to build a robust battery recycling industry, which in turn boosts the demand for caustic soda.

#tgvsraac TGV Sraac Chooses Thyssenkrupp Nucera for Plant Expansion

📢 Indian caustic soda major TGV Sraac has once again partnered with thyssenkrupp nucera for a significant plant expansion in July 2025 ! This project will increase production capacity by 50%, from 1,000 to 1,500 tonnes per day, using state-of-the-art energy-efficient technology. This move aligns with India's push for sustainable, self-sufficient manufacturing and strengthens the domestic market.

India is better positioned with early action. EV battery waste remains low now (~1kT in 2025; total Li-ion EOL ~36kT, mostly consumer electronics). 

Battery Waste Management Rules 2022 set EPR with rising targets: 90% material recovery for EV batteries by 2026-27, plus recycled content mandates (up to 20% by 2030). 

Formal capacity ~60k TPA and expanding (Attero, Lohum, others). NITI projects ~59 GWh EV waste by 2030. Strong push for circular economy and critical mineral recycling to reduce import dependence. 

Proactive rules give India an edge to scale sustainably.

♻️ INDIA'S RECYCLING ECONOMY | A Multi-Decade Investment Theme? 🇮🇳

India is moving rapidly towards a circular economy, where waste is no longer discarded but converted into valuable raw materials. Rising commodity prices, stricter environmental regulations, urbanisation, EV adoption, and government initiatives are creating long-term opportunities across the recycling ecosystem.

Instead of viewing recycling as a traditional business, investors are increasingly tracking it as an emerging structural growth theme.

🔋 Lead Recycling

🔹 Gravita India – One of India's largest lead recyclers with a growing presence in aluminium, plastic and rubber recycling.

🔹 Pondy Oxides & Chemicals – Major player in lead recycling and lead alloy manufacturing.

🔹 Nile Ltd – Lead recycling and battery material business catering to industrial demand.

---

♻️ Metal Recycling

🔹 Nupur Recyclers – Processes ferrous and non-ferrous scrap for industrial applications.

🔹 Jain Resource Recycling – Focused on recovering valuable metals from industrial waste.

🔹 Baheti Recycling Industries – Aluminium recycling benefiting from increasing demand for lightweight metals.

---

🗑️ Waste Management & Municipal Services

🔹 MSTC – Government-backed e-auction platform facilitating scrap disposal and resource recovery.

🔹 Antony Waste Handling Cell – Integrated municipal solid waste (MSW) collection, processing and landfill management.

🔹 A2Z Infra Engineering – Waste collection, transportation and municipal waste processing.

🔹 Urban Enviro Waste Management – Solid waste management services across multiple cities.

---

💧 Water Recycling & Sewage Treatment

🔹 VA Tech Wabag – Global leader in water treatment, desalination and wastewater recycling.

🔹 EMS Ltd – Sewage treatment plants (STPs), water supply and wastewater infrastructure projects.

🔹 Felix Industries – Industrial wastewater treatment and water recycling solutions.

---

🛞 Tyre & Rubber Recycling

🔹 GRP Ltd – Recycled rubber products with domestic and export markets.

🔹 Elgi Rubber Company – Tyre retreading and rubber recycling solutions.

🔹 Tinna Rubber & Infrastructure – Converts waste tyres into crumb rubber and modified bitumen.

---

🧴 Plastic & PET Recycling

🔹 Ganesha Ecosphere – India's leading PET bottle recycling company producing recycled polyester fibre.

🔹 Race Eco Chain – Plastic waste collection and recycling with a focus on sustainability.

---

💻 E-Waste Recycling

🔹 Cerebra Integrated Technologies – IT asset management and electronic waste recycling.

🔹 Eco Recycling (Ecoreco) – One of India's oldest organised e-waste recyclers.

🔹 Namo eWaste Management – End-to-end e-waste collection, processing and recycling.

📈 Why This Theme Could Grow Over the Next Decade

✅ India's circular economy policy push
✅ Rapid increase in electronic waste generation
✅ Higher recycling targets across industries
✅ Growing demand for recycled metals and plastics
✅ Rising battery recycling opportunity from EV adoption
✅ Water scarcity driving wastewater recycling projects
✅ ESG investing

India's recycling industry is moving from a niche business to a strategic growth sector.

With rising e-waste, battery usage, plastic consumption and stricter environmental regulations, recycling is becoming an important pillar of India's circular economy.

Government incentives, EPR (Extended Producer Responsibility) rules, critical mineral recovery and sustainability goals are creating long-term opportunities for organized recycling companies.

📰 Latest Sector Update

The Government has approved a 1,500-crore incentive scheme to promote recycling of critical minerals from e-waste and end-of-life products, aiming to strengthen domestic supply chains & reduce import dependence. 
This is expected to benefit the organized recycling ecosystem over the coming years.

📈 Why this theme matters

♻️ India is rapidly adopting a circular economy.
🔋 EV battery recycling demand is accelerating.
💻 E-waste generation continues to grow every year.
🏭 Industrial metal recycling is becoming increasingly important.
🌍 ESG investing is driving capital toward sustainable businesses.
🧲 Critical mineral recovery has become a strategic priority.
🚛 Organized waste management is replacing the unorganized sector.
🏛️ Government policies continue to support recycling infrastructure.

👀 Key Triggers to Watch

✅ Critical mineral recycling incentives.
✅ EPR policy implementation.
✅ Battery recycling expansion.
✅ Aluminium & lead recycling demand.
✅ E-waste collection growth.
✅ Capacity expansion.
✅ Commodity price trends.
✅ Export opportunities.
✅ New recycling technologies.
✅ Circular economy initiatives.

🔍 Recent Company Updates

🔹 Gravita India – Continues expanding its lead, aluminium and plastic recycling operations across multiple countries while increasing capacity in value-added recycled products.

🔹 Eco Recycling (Ecoreco) – One of India's pioneers in e-waste recycling, benefiting from tighter EPR compliance and rising demand for responsible electronic waste management.

🔹 Antony Waste Handling Cell – Continues to benefit from increasing municipal solid waste management projects as Indian cities expand organized waste processing infrastructure.

🔹 Ganesha Ecosphere – Remains India's leading PET bottle recycler, supported by growing demand for recycled polyester from global apparel and textile brands.

🔹 Baheti Recycling Industries – Expanding its aluminium recycling business to capitalize on increasing demand for recycled non-ferrous metals.

🔹 CMR Green Technologies – Recently made a strong stock market debut, underscoring investor interest in India's aluminium recycling sector.

🔹 VA Tech Wabag – Continues winning industrial and municipal water treatment projects, supporting long-term wastewater recycling growth.

🔹 Ion Exchange (India) – Expanding water treatment and industrial water recycling solutions across domestic and international markets.

India's recycling industry is no longer just about waste management it's becoming a strategic sector linked to resource security, sustainability, critical minerals & green manufacturing. 
Companies with strong recycling capabilities could benefit as the circular economy continues to expand.

Most of us want to recycle. We just don't always know how.

Today, that became a little easier.

New Delhi Municipal Council (NDMC), in collaboration with Chintan Environmental Research and Action Group, launched the Waste Wise Citizens handbook—a practical guide that translates the Solid Waste Management Rules, 2026 into simple, everyday actions. From waste segregation to understanding what can be recycled, the handbook helps residents make informed choices that reduce waste and recover valuable resources.

The day also marked the flagging off of RRR on Wheels, a doorstep collection initiative that makes it easier for households to responsibly dispose of segregated dry waste, e-waste, textiles and other recyclable materials. At the same time, it strengthens the livelihoods of trained waste-pickers, who play a vital role in keeping resources in circulation.

Together, the handbook and RRR on Wheels make it easier for citizens to turn good intentions into everyday action—bringing Delhi one step closer to a cleaner, more circular future.

Detailed Analysis on the business of Tinna Rubber and Infrastructure - The largest integrated end-of-life Tyre Recycler in India.

Recycling of automobile tyres:
This problem can be solved by recycling tyres.
Recycled tyres can now be used as playground chips, athletic fields, and in construction.

Because of the tough materials used in tyres, recycled tyre waste can be an excellent candidate for concrete replacement. 
Currently, the size of the estimated rubber and tyre recycling industry in India is around ₹35 Billion.

We arranged a site visit for Tinna Rubber & Infrastructure Ltd at their Wada facility in Palghar. One of the more interesting circular economy plays in India. 

Notes -

Detailed Blog link - https://t.co/ZxdlVRx4fb

The ELT Problem (and Opportunity) - The world generates ~1–1.5 billion end-of-life tyres (ELTs) every year — nearly 14.8 million tonnes of rubber entering the waste stream annually. Global stockpiles already sit at ~4 billion tyres, projected to hit 5 billion by 2030. What was once purely a disposal headache is now a resource opportunity, as circular economy model gains ground and regulations tighten around tyre waste.

What Tinna Does - India's largest producer of Crumb Rubber, CRM, and MRP — and one of Asia's leading ELT recyclers. The model is simple in concept but complex in execution: collect waste tyres, shred and process them, and convert the output into high-value materials used across infrastructure, steel, industrials, consumer goods, and polymer composites. 

Core Business & Capacity - Tinna's flagship product is Crumb Rubber Modified Bitumen (CRMB) — rubberised bitumen used in road construction that blends 14–15% recycled rubber. Current tyre crushing capacity stands at 1.85 lakh TPA (split between TBR and PCR tyres) and is being scaled up ~35% to 2.5 lakh TPA by FY27, with ₹79 Cr capex already deployed in 9M FY26.

International Operations - Tinna is quietly building a global footprint across three geographies. 

- Oman, their JV Global Recycle LLC is running at ~80% capacity, contributing ~₹25 Cr to 9M FY26 revenue, 
 - South Africa, JV Mbodla Investments has secured approval to export 24,000 ELTs to India, with Phase 1 operational and Phase 2 targets full-scale crumb rubber production by Q1 FY27. 
- In Saudi Arabia, Tinna Rubber Arabia Ltd. is setting up a 24,000 MTPA plant with land allocated.

What to Watch - Vision 2028 targets ~₹1,000 Cr revenue, 25%+ CAGR, 18%+ EBITDA margins, and 30%+ ROCE. EPR regulations remain a structural tailwind. The key monitorable is execution and international ops turning profitable. Interesting space, but the next 2–3 quarters of volume data will be telling.

#TINNARUBR #CircularEconomy #TyreRecycling #IndiaStocks

--------------------------------------------

India generates 2.5–3 MT of waste tyres every year

Here's a comprehensive analysis of the key achievements and initiatives of India's Ministry of Environment, Forest and Climate Change in 2024:

Major Environmental Initiatives and Achievements

1. "Ek Ped Maa Ke Naam" Campaign

- Launched on World Environment Day (June 5, 2024) by the Prime Minister
- Aim: Plant 140 crore trees by March 2025 as a tribute to mothers and Mother Earth
- Progress: Over 102 crore trees planted so far

2. Eco-mark Scheme

- Notified on September 26, 2024, replacing the 1991 scheme
- Objectives:
  - Encourage demand for environmentally friendly products
  - Promote lower energy consumption and resource efficiency
  - Support circular economy principles
  - Ensure accurate product labeling

3. Climate Action and Net Zero Goals

- India committed to net-zero emissions by 2070
- Achievements:
  - 33% reduction in emission intensity of GDP between 2005-2019
  - 46.52% of total installed electric power capacity from non-fossil fuel sources (as of October 31, 2024)
- Updated NDC targets:
  - 45% reduction in emission intensity by 2030
  - 50% cumulative electric power capacity from non-fossil fuels by 2030

4. National Clean Air Programme (NCAP)

- Launched in 2019, covering 130 cities across 24 states/UTs
- Objective: 40% reduction in particulate matter by 2025-26 from 2017-18 levels
- Achievements:
  - Over 20% reduction in particulate matter in 55 cities
  - 40% reduction achieved in 23 cities
  - 18 cities now meeting national ambient air quality standards
- Funding: ₹11,200 crore allocated for air pollution mitigation measures
- PRANA portal launched for real-time air quality monitoring

5. Circular Economy and Extended Producer Responsibility (EPR)

- EPR rules notified for various waste streams:
  - Plastic waste
  - Tyre waste
  - Battery waste
  - Used oil waste
  - E-waste
- EPR framework under consideration for:
  - Scrap metals
  - End-of-life vehicles
  - Solid and liquid waste
  - Construction and demolition waste

6. India Cooling Action Plan (ICAP)

- Action plans finalized for six thematic areas
- Achievement: 50% reduction in Hydrochlorofluorocarbons (HCFCs) consumption, surpassing the 35% target
- HCFC phase-out in new equipment manufacturing by December 31, 2024

7. Mangrove Initiative for Shoreline Habitats & Tangible Incomes (MISHTI)

- Launched on June 5, 2024
- Objective: Restore and promote mangrove ecosystems
- Funding: ₹100 crore initial allocation through CAMPA
- Progress:
  - 22,561 hectares of degraded mangrove area restored in 13 states/UTs
  - ₹17.96 crore released for restoring 3,836 hectares in 6 states/UTs

Answer: (c) All the three
Statement 1: Cigarette butts
Are cigarette butts non-biodegradable? Yes, they are largely non-biodegradable.
Explanation: Cigarette filters are mainly made of cellulose acetate, a type of plastic. This material does not degrade easily in the environment and can persist for years, causing pollution.
Though cellulose acetate is a form of cellulose, it is heavily treated and has low biodegradability.
Environmental Impact: Cigarette butts are among the most common litter items worldwide and pose a significant threat to marine and terrestrial ecosystems due to their slow degradation and release of toxic chemicals.

Supporting Source: Central Pollution Control Board (CPCB), India – Reports on plastic pollution and waste management. Environmental Protection Agency (EPA) USA – Facts on cigarette butt pollution. NCERT Class 12 Environmental Studies (Waste Management chapter).

Statement 2: Eyeglass lenses
Are eyeglass lenses non-biodegradable? Yes, they are non-biodegradable.
Explanation: Eyeglass lenses are commonly made from polycarbonate plastic or glass. Polycarbonate plastic is a synthetic polymer and is non-biodegradable. Glass, though natural, does not biodegrade but is inert and can last indefinitely unless physically broken down. Since eyeglass lenses do not decompose naturally by biological agents, they are classified as non-biodegradable waste.
Supporting Source: Ministry of Environment, Forest and Climate Change (MoEFCC), India – Guidelines on plastic and e-waste. Scientific literature on polymer waste biodegradability. NCERT Environmental Studies.

Statement 3: Car tyres
Are car tyres non-biodegradable? Yes, they are non-biodegradable.
Explanation: Car tyres are made primarily of synthetic rubber, carbon black, and other chemical additives. These materials do not decompose naturally and persist in the environment for decades.
Discarded tyres create serious environmental problems, including breeding grounds for mosquitoes and fire hazards.

Supporting Source: CPCB India – Reports on tyre waste and recycling. United Nations Environment Programme (UNEP) – Waste management documents. NCERT Environment chapter.

quotedTweet.text:
In Depth Analysis of Recycling Theme ♻️ A thread 🧵 👉

EPR, which stands for Extended Producer Responsibility, is a fundamental requirement for Producers, Importers, and Brand Owners (PIBOs) involved in the generation of Plastic, Battery, or e-waste in India.

❇️How effective is EPR?

EPR policies have been known to contribute to packaging waste reduction and increase recycling activities. EPR policies are having a positive impact throughout the years in many countries.

❇️How EPR Works?

The liability of extended producer responsibility gets passed on from the seller of a commodity to its
purchaser – in this case, plastic packaging –to avoid duplication in accounting. It means that when a producer buys plastic from an importer, and then directly places the product on the market, the liability to collect back and recycle falls on the producer.

Similarly, if a brand owner buys plastic from a producer or an importer and uses it to place its products on the market, the brand owner becomes liable for end-of-life management of that amount of plastic waste.

The collection target (also known as EPR target) is arrived at by calculating the average of the amount of the plastic that a polluter has placed on the market in the last two consecutive years. 

EPR is ‘brand neutral’ – this
means that a polluter does not necessarily limit itself to its own plastic packaging while collecting back, it can collect and recycle plastics put on the market by any other brand to fulfil its EPR target

❇️Note-The EPR certificates can only be issued to the ‘polluters’ by authorised plastic waste processors/recyclers.

If you like the thread, Please bookmark and retweet 🔁

#EPR #RecyclingTheme

In a landmark move, the Commissionerate of Food Safety, Kerala will now fine eateries up to ₹1 lakh for reusing cooking oil more than three times — unless it’s handed over to authorised agencies.

Why? Because reused oil = toxic oil.

It’s been linked to cancer, heart disease, and other serious health risks.

But here’s the good news:
Under the RUCO (Repurpose Used Cooking Oil) scheme and Eat Right India, used oil can be collected and converted into biodiesel — reducing waste and protecting your health.

In Kozhikode alone, 100+ eateries already hand over 10,000+ litres every month.
Hotels and bakeries using over 50L oil/day must now comply and maintain registers.

This isn’t just regulation.

It’s a step toward cleaner fuel, safer food, and a healthier India.

Should all states adopt this? Tell us below. 👇

#EatRightIndia #FoodSafety #Kerala #PublicHealth #HealthyIndia #BioDiesel #Health #FoodLaws

Participated in the Resource Efficiency & Circular Economy Industry Coalition (RECEIC) Global Symposium on Resource Efficiency & Circular Economy in New Delhi. 

It was energising to witness such a rich convergence of industry leaders, policymakers, and global partners under one roof, all aligned around one shared purpose: moving circularity from intent to action.

The day opened with some thought provoking discussions on decarbonising the chemicals sector, with the release of a knowledge paper on Used Cooking Oil as a renewable feedstock, a real and scalable solution that the working group has been diligently building towards. The conversation on built infrastructure was equally compelling, with participants highlighting how the material and design choices being made today will shape India's carbon trajectory for decades to come.

The Inaugural Session was graced by the august presence of Dr. Jitendra Singh, Hon'ble Union Minister of State (Independent Charge) for Science & Technology and Earth Sciences, as the Chief Guest. He delivered a powerful keynote, reinforcing the need for a mindset shift of all the stakeholders towards building a sustainable future. 

We were also privileged to have Shri Neelesh Sah, Joint Secretary, Ministry of Environment, Forests & Climate Change, Government of India, whose address reinforced the government's strong commitment to embedding circularity into India's policy and regulatory frameworks.

There were focused discussions on strengthening India's used oil ecosystem, making eco-labelling work for MSMEs, and bridging the gap between packaging innovation and large-scale market adoption. 

It was also wonderful to see participants from across the country and overseas, bringing diverse perspectives and a genuine commitment to the cause. The RECEIC Awards added a special touch to the day, recognising organisations and individuals who have made sustainability a central agenda.

Events like these are a reminder that the momentum around circular economy in India is real, growing, and increasingly backed by both industry conviction and policy intent. The journey from discussion to deployment is well and truly underway.

MAK Lubricants has signed Memoranda of Understanding (MoUs) with M/s Siyaram Petrotech Pvt. Ltd. and M/s Vzen Industries Pvt. Ltd. to explore collaboration in building a sustainable Extended Producer Responsibility (EPR) Used Oil Ecosystem in India.

The partnership will focus on strengthening used oil collection, aggregation, digital tracking, re-refining, and responsible management, while exploring opportunities in Re-Refined Base Oil (RRBO), EPR compliance, and advanced re-refining technologies.

This collaboration reinforces BPCL's commitment to sustainability, circular economy principles, and responsible lubricant lifecycle management.

FDA intensifies action on unsafe festive food; helpline open for public complaints

Ahead of the Diwali festival, the Food and Drug Administration (FDA) of Maharashtra issued guidelines for food manufacturers and sellers to ensure festive food items like sweets, khoya, paneer, ghee, and dry fruits are safe and free from adulteration.

With demand soaring during the festival, the FDA has ramped up inspections of food establishments to curb the sale of substandard or unsafe products. It has also set up a helpline number for the public to file complaints.

“The festive season brings an increase in the demand for traditional food items, and with it, the risk of adulteration. We are conducting thorough inspections to ensure that food businesses comply with safety standards. Strict action will be taken against those found violating the Food Safety and Standards Act,” Joint Commissioner (Food), Greater Mumbai, Mangesh Mane, said.

The guideline issued by the FDA include maintaining clean and hygienic production environments, using only potable water, and ensuring that raw materials are sourced from licensed suppliers.

“Manufacturers must follow stringent hygiene practices. Food workers must undergo medical check-ups, and only food-grade colors should be used,” said Mane, stressing the FDA’s zero-tolerance approach to violations.

In addition to cleanliness and proper storage practices, the FDA has cautioned manufacturers against reusing frying oil, a practice known to degrade food quality.

“Reusing oil can result in harmful effects on health. We are ensuring that businesses hand over used oil to authorised aggregators,” Mane added.

Mane urged the public to remain cautious while purchasing food items, saying, “We request consumers to check for essential details such as the batch number, use-by date, and FSSAI license number on packaging before buying. If they come across any suspicious or substandard products, we encourage them to report it immediately.”

Look closely at the timeline, and the pattern becomes difficult to ignore. India imports more than 85% of its crude oil and accounts for 30% of oil demand growth globally. 

On 5 June, India took a major step towards reducing that dependence by launching E85 fuel for flex-fuel vehicles at Rs 20/litre cheaper than normal E20 fuel, along with the launch of flex fuel cars and bikes. 

Soon after, a coordinated controversy around ethanol-blended fuels began to dominate the conversation and fearmongering started.

Consider the facts. E15+ has been used on Indian roads for over three years. E19+ for over 2 years. E20 has been available since April 2025. More than 20 crore two-wheelers and over 20 lakh four-wheelers have been running successfully on E20 fuel for nearly two years. 

Yet, as India accelerated its journey towards greater energy self-reliance, opposition to ethanol blending suddenly intensified.

It is worth asking: who benefits if India remains dependent on imported crude oil or batteries. 

Every litre of ethanol blended into petrol helps reduce crude oil imports, strengthens India's energy security, reduces air pollution, supports domestic farmers, and saves valuable foreign exchange. Greater use of indigenous fuels also makes the country less vulnerable to geopolitical disruptions and global price shocks. 

E20 is a widely tested, verified, scientific, internationally proven and completely safe fuel for our vehicles. It is also a completely Atmanirbhar fuel which has made our “Annadatas” into “Urjadatas”.

Are we still in the #BritishEra❓❓

🏙️ Pune, Mumbai & many Indian cities are still relying on chimney norms rooted in the #1947s

📏 A 30.5 m chimney made sense when nearby buildings were only ~12 m (2–3 storeys) tall.

🏢 In 2026, many surrounding buildings are ~70 m or taller.

📐 If the guideline is that the chimney should be 2.5× the height of nearby buildings,
the required chimney height would be around 175–200 m,
not 30.5 m.

⚠️ Yet, outdated 30.5 m norms continue to be applied in dense urban areas..
ignoring today's skyline and dispersion realities.

GD अग्रवाल नाम है इनका। 

ज़रा गूगल करके या जैमिनी से चैट जीपीटी से जिससे पूछना होगा, पूछ लीजिएगा। ग्रेटा थनबर्ग, सोनम वांगचुक जैसे तमाम लोगों में इनकी गिनती होती थी। 

जो हिंदुस्तान कहिये या यूं कहिए प्रकृति को बचाना चाहता था। और 111 दिन के अनशन के बाद इसने अपने प्राण त्याग दिए। 

बस एक छोटी सी ख़बर बनी। इस देश का मीडिया और उन्मादी लोग इस महान विचारक को खा गए। इनकी पढ़ाई देखिएगा ये कोई बाबा नहीं थे। 

उच्च शिक्षा: उन्होंने IIT रुड़की (तब रुड़की यूनिवर्सिटी) से सिविल इंजीनियरिंग की और फिर दुनिया के सबसे प्रतिष्ठित संस्थानों में से एक, कैलिफोर्निया विश्वविद्यालय, बर्कले से पर्यावरण इंजीनियरिंग में Ph.D. की।

अकादमिक और प्रशासनिक कद: वह IIT कानपुर में पर्यावरण इंजीनियरिंग विभाग के प्रमुख (HOD) रहे। इसके साथ ही, वह भारत के केंद्रीय प्रदूषण नियंत्रण बोर्ड (CPCB) के पहले सदस्य सचिव (Member Secretary) भी थे।

कभी दिमाग़ फिर गया होगा तो सोचा इस देश को बचा लें। तो गंगा को बचाने के लिए अनशन पर बैठ गए। 

लेकिन हमको गंगा तो बचानी नहीं थी। हम पता नहीं क्या ही बचा रहे थे? जो न ख़त्म हुआ था। न ख़त्म होगा। लेकिन हमको बचाने का ज़िम्मा दिया गया था सो सब बचा ही रहे थे। इसलिए गंगा के नाम पर धंधा हुआ कि उसको बचाना है। लेकिन बचाया नहीं गया जेबें ज़रूर भर गई। 

और एक शख़्स इस दुनिया से चला गया इस देश को बचाने के लिए। हम सब मरेंगे क्योंकि हमने सही लोगों को कभी नहीं पहचाना। हम बस दलालों को सिर आंखों पर बिठाकर रखते हैं। 

जिस इंसान के पास आराम, शोहरत और विदेश में बसने के तमाम रास्ते खुले थे, उसने सब कुछ छोड़कर गंगा और प्रकृति के लिए लड़ना चुना।

111 दिनों का महा-अनशन और व्यवस्था की बेरुखी। साल 2018 में, 86 वर्ष की आयु में, उन्होंने गंगा को प्रदूषण मुक्त करने, उस पर बन रहे बड़े बांधों को रुकवाने और 'गंगा महाधुत कानून' पास कराने के लिए हरिद्वार में अनशन शुरू किया।

111 दिनों तक वह केवल पानी और शहद पर रहे। एक ऐसा वैज्ञानिक, जिसने देश के विकास और पर्यावरण की नीतियों को गढ़ा था, वह घुट-घुट कर मर रहा था।  मुख्यधारा की मीडिया के लिए यह कोई बड़ी खबर नहीं थी। वे धार्मिक ध्रुवीकरण, राजनीतिक नूराकुश्ती और खोखले राष्ट्रवाद को बेचने में व्यस्त थे।

जीडी अग्रवाल मर गए, वो भी विदेश में रह सकते थे लेकिन उन्होंने एक ऐसा कृतघ्न देश चुना जो उनका उपकार तक न माने, वहां उन्होंने लड़ते - लड़ते मरना चुना।

The Great Indian Pollution Mega Show!

📅05/11/2023 

🟢  #Fatehabad in #Haryana tops the most polluted city list in India on 05/11/2023 with PM2.5 concentration at 338 µg/m3. 

🟢   #Delhi was the 2nd most polluted city in India on 05/11/2023 with PM2.5 concentration at 334 µg/m3, where Graded Response Action Plan (GRAP) stage IV has been activated with further curbs on activities leading to higher emission load, i.e., the introduction of ODD EVEN Scheme (but it will start from 13th November to 20th November) is this just a gesture as pollution levels are rising now and strict actions are needed now.

🟢   For comparison, India’s own NAAQS stipulates the daily standard at 60 µg/m3, and WHO guideline levels are 15 µg/m3.

🟢   7 out of the top 10 polluted cities in India are in Delhi-NCR, #Fatehabad, #Kaithal, and #Hisar were the other three cities from Haryana which featured in the top 10 polluted cities list & aren’t included in the Delhi-NCR region.

CPCB.text:
Did You Know?

OCEMS enables continuous online monitoring of effluent quality from applicable industrial units. The system helps regulatory authorities monitor compliance through real-time data transmission.
#OCEMS

It's Not Just Monitoring

OCEMS helps industries demonstrate compliance while enabling DPCC to monitor effluent quality remotely through continuous online data received from applicable units.
#EnvironmentalGovernance

Fact Check

OCEMS is not mandatory for every industry. It is applicable to specified industrial units covered under CPCB and DPCC directions, based on prescribed regulatory criteria.
#KnowTheRules

'6th International Day of Clean Air for Blue Skies' at NIT Rourkela!

The Sustainable Development Goals (SDG) Committee, NIT Rourkela, State Pollution Control Board Rourkela Regional Office and Rourkela Municipal Corporation jointly organised a series of events during September 05-07, 2025, to mark the observance of the '6th International Day of Clean Air for Blue Skies'. The three-day program featured a series of events, including research poster presentation, painting, quizzes, and a case study competition with the participation of more than 200 students from different institutes, including BPUT Rourkela and VSSUT Burla.  In order to spread awareness among the school children, a hackathon on the theme was also organised, which brought together over 40 students from various schools in Rourkela, showcasing innovative ideas. Together, they proposed practical solutions to control air pollution and sustainability challenges while fostering curiosity, teamwork, and a genuine passion for learning. The SDG Campus Club and YMFI Rourkela assisted in conducting the events.

The main event, orgainsed on 7th September, was graced by Chief Guest Dr. Subhankar Moahapatra (IAS, District Magistrate and Collector, Sundargarh) along with Prof. K Umamaheshwar Rao (Director, NIT Rourkela) and Ms. Pallavi Nayak (Joint Commissioner, RMC Rourkela) as the distinguished guests and Shri Sanjeev Pal (General Manager, Environment, Tata Steel, Meramundali, Dhenkanal) as the technical speaker. More than 240 participants from different industries, colleges and schools, along with several dignitaries, including Shri M. P. Singh {Executive Director (Mines)}, Shri B. K. Jojo (CGM, Town Engg., SAIL-Rourkela Steel Plant), Shri R. S. Raghuwanshi (Associate Vice President, Jindal Steel Ltd), and Shri  Ajit Pattnaik (Asst Commissioner RMC, Rourkela). Prof. H. B. Sahu {Dean(AIIR) and Chairman of the SDG Committee-NITR}, and Dr. A. K. Mallick (Additional Chief Environmental Scientist and Regional Officer, SPCB Rourkela), were the Organising Chairs of the event.  

Drawing insightful parallels from the epic Mahabharata, Dr Mohapatra, Chief Guest, emphasized the importance of harmonising developmental aspirations with ecological preservation. He also highlighted the pivotal role of academic institutions like NIT Rourkela in fostering environmental awareness, advancing research, and driving technological innovation. He pledged his support for any novel ideas or initiatives from faculty and students that aim to improve the quality of life and promote a cleaner, healthier future. Prof. Bhisma Tyagi also delivered an insightful presentation on 'Air Quality Status and Impacts for India'. Prof. Bikash Chandra Maharaj, Member of the SDG Committee, proposed the vote of thanks. The event was seamlessly anchored by Sri Raghuraj Somani and Ritika Mallick, members of the SDG Campus Club and was supported by Shri Bibekananda Das, General Secretary, YMFI, Rourkela. 

Concluding the event, the audience appreciated the commendable efforts of the SDG Committee, NIT Rourkela, as it has been ranked 9th among SDG-focused institutions nationwide by NIRF 2025, an achievement that distinguishes it as the only NIT to earn this honour.

#Sustainability #SustainableDevelopment #nitrourkela #cleanair #bluesky #environmentalawareness

■ What are the changes in the new Water Act?

✅The Lok Sabha recently approved the Water (Prevention and Control of Pollution) Amendment Act, 2024, which was earlier passed in the Rajya Sabha.

✅This amendment revises the Water (Prevention and Control of Pollution) Act, 1974, marking significant changes in India's approach to water pollution management.

✅The amended Act  will currently apply to Himachal Pradesh, Rajasthan and Union territories.

● Water (Prevention and Control of Pollution) Act, 1974

✅The Act, enacted in 1974, aimed to establish institutional mechanisms for addressing contamination of water bodies in India.

✅It led to the formation of the Central Pollution Control Boards (CPCB) and State Pollution Control Boards (SPCB) in September 1974.

✅To monitor and prevent contamination of water bodies by industrial effluents and sewage.

✅Industrial units were required to obtain permission from State boards before factory  establishment, ensuring compliance with prescribed norms.

● Amendments to the Act:

✅It replaces imprisonment penalties for certain violations,  deemed “minor”, with fines ranging from ₹10,000 to ₹15 lakh.

✅The Centre gains increased authority to override SPCB in some instances and exempt industrial plants from certain permissions, consulting with the CPCB.

✅As per the original Act, the SPCB’s permission is needed for establishing any industry or treatment plant, which could discharge sewage into a water body, sewer, or land.

✅Violations such as operating without SPCB consent still carry a penalty of up to six years of imprisonment along with fines.

✅It also penalises tampering with monitoring devices used in determining whether any industry or treatment plant can be set up.

✅The Centre is empowered to issue guidelines for the grant, refusal, or cancellation of consent granted by the SPCB.

✅The Centre can also establish rules for selecting SPCB chairpersons and provide guidelines for State boards on industry establishment procedures.

● Response to Amendments:

✅The Environment Minister defended the amendments stating that outdated regulations caused a "trust deficit" and harassment to businesses.

✅The Opposition members expressed concerns that the amendments weaken protections against industrial pollution of rivers and water bodies.

✅They argued that the fear of imprisonment acted as an effective deterrent to industrial units that were lax with complying with strict regulations.

SOURCE - THE HINDU

நீர்இன்று அமையாது உலகெனின் யார்யார்க்கும் வான்இன்று அமையாது ஒழுக்கு

மனித உயிர்வாழ்வதற்கு நீர் எவ்வளவு இன்றியமையாதது என்பதைக் கண்டறிவது எளிது. குடிப்பதற்கும், சுத்தம் செய்வதற்கும், கழுவுவதற்கும், சமைப்பதற்கும், பொழுதுபோக்கு நோக்கங்களுக்காகவும் குடிநீரைப் பயன்படுத்துகிறோம். இயற்கையில் நீர் ஏராளமாக இருந்தாலும், மனித நடவடிக்கைகள் நீர் மாசுபாட்டின் காரணமாக பெரும்பாலான இயற்கை நீர் ஆதாரங்களைப் பயன்படுத்த முடியாததாக ஆக்கியுள்ளன, பெரும்பாலான சந்தர்ப்பங்களில் சுத்திகரிக்கப்பட்ட மற்றும் பாட்டில் தண்ணீரைக் குடிப்பதைத் தவிர வேறு வழிகள் இல்லை. 

சுத்திகரிக்கப்படாத தண்ணீரைக் குடிப்பதால் ஏற்படும் உடல்நலப் பிரச்சினைகள் குறித்த விழிப்புணர்வு அதிகரித்துள்ளது, இது பாதுகாப்பான தொகுக்கப்பட்ட குடிநீரின் மீதான நம்பிக்கையை மேலும் அதிகரித்துள்ளது. எனவே இன்று நீர் பாட்டில் ஆலைகளுக்கான தேவை மிகப்பெரியது. ரோ வாட்டர், மினரல் வாட்டர் போன்ற குடிநீரை பாட்டில் செய்வதில் ஈடுபடும் வணிகங்கள் நாட்டின் ஒவ்வொரு பகுதியிலும் அதிகரித்துள்ளன. கனரக உலோகங்கள், தீங்கு விளைவிக்கும் இரசாயனங்கள், மைக்ரோபிளாஸ்டிக்ஸ் போன்ற அசுத்தங்களைக் கொண்ட நிலத்தடி நீர்த்தேக்கங்கள் மாசுபடுவதால், குடிப்பதற்கு ஏற்ற நிலத்தடி நீர் இன்று அவ்வாறு இல்லை.

இன்று இந்த தேவை ஒரு பரந்த தொழிலாக மாற்றப்பட்டுள்ளது, இது பாதுகாப்பான குடிநீரை பேக்கேஜ் செய்து வழங்குகிறது. நீர் பாட்டில் ஆலை அதிக லாப வரம்புகளை வழங்குகிறது மற்றும் இலவச வளத்தைப் பயன்படுத்துகிறது. தேவையான அனைத்தும் நீர் விநியோகத்தின் சுத்தமான ஆதாரம், ஒரு வடிகட்டுதல் அமைப்பு மற்றும் ஒரு பாட்டில் ஆலை, நீங்கள் செல்ல உள்ளீர்கள். பாட்டில் மற்றும் பேக்கேஜ் செய்யப்பட்ட தண்ணீருக்கான தேவை இன்று மிகப் பெரியதாக இருப்பதால், சிறிய பன்வாலாக்கள் முதல் உணவகங்கள் மற்றும் உணவகங்கள் வரை மற்ற எல்லா கடைகளிலும் மினரல் வாட்டர் பாட்டில்களைக் காணலாம். தண்ணீரைச் சுத்திகரிக்க பல்வேறு முறைகள் பயன்படுத்தப்படுகின்றன, மேலும் தயாரிப்புகளின் இறுதி கலவை சட்டங்களால் கட்டாயப்படுத்தப்பட்ட மிகவும் கடுமையான விதிமுறைகளுக்கு இணங்குவதை உறுதிசெய்ய செயல்முறைகளின் பல சேர்க்கைகள் இணைக்கப்பட்டுள்ளன. 

தண்ணீர் வியாபாரம் என்பது வியாபாரம் மட்டுமல்ல, இன்று தேவையும் கூட. நகர்ப்புற மக்கள் தொகை அதிகரித்துள்ளதால் சுத்தமான தண்ணீருக்கான தேவை அதிகரித்து வருகிறது. குறைந்த அளவிலான நன்னீர் ஆதாரங்களுடன், மினரல் வாட்டர் வடிவில் குடிநீர் மற்றும் சுத்தமான குடிநீருக்கான தேவை அதிகரிப்பதற்கும், வீடுகள், அலுவலகங்கள், பள்ளிகள், தொழில் நிறுவனங்கள் போன்றவற்றுக்கான நீர் தேவைகளை வழங்கும் வணிகத்திற்கும் நிலைமை வழிவகுத்தது.

பாட்டில் தண்ணீர் வணிகத்தைத் தொடங்குவதற்கான நிலைகள்

பாட்டில் ஆலை அமைப்பில் உள்ள நிலைகள் மற்றும் அதில் உள்ள செலவைப் புரிந்து கொள்ள, நீர் சேமிப்பு, சுத்திகரிப்பு மற்றும் பேக்கேஜிங் அமைப்பு உள்ளிட்ட யூனிட்டின் செயல்பாடுகளைப் புரிந்துகொள்வது அவசியம். தண்ணீர் பாட்டில் ஆலையில் ஈடுபட்டுள்ள சுத்திகரிப்பு மற்றும் பேக்கேஜிங் அமைப்புகளுக்கு பின்வருபவை தேவைப்படும்.

Stages in Starting a Bottled Water Business

To understand the stages involved in the bottling plant setup and the cost involved, it is necessary to understand the activities in the unit, including water storage, treatment and packaging system. The treatment and packaging systems involved in the water bottling plant will need the following.

💧Raw Water Pump
💧Dual Media filter
💧Carbon Filter
💧Antiscalant Dosing System
💧Micron Cartridge filter
💧Pretreatment
💧High-pressure Pump
💧Reverse osmosis pump
💧UV system
💧Ozone System

The following stage is essential for starting a bottling plant business

Stage 1: Planning Your Water Bottling Plant Business
This stage will need the entrepreneur to decide on the following aspects

Location: location is a crucial factor that can determine the profitability of the water bottling business. The proximity to a water source and good connectivity through the road is essential for capturing a larger market share.

Machinery: requirement for machinery is almost the same as mentioned above. However, with the variation in capacity, the entrepreneur will need higher-capacity machinery, thereby adding to the initial cost of the plant setup.

Space and power requirement: the space required to set up the whole unit will depend on the quantities of water that will be treated and bottled. This will, in turn, rely on the demand in the nearby market and the presence of raw material (i.e. water). There are four sections in the bottling plant: water treatment, bottling, quality control (lab) and overall utility. Generally, a standard 2000 LPH packaged bottling plant needs 5000 Sq. Ft built space with 3000 Sq. Ft of covered area. The power required will be around 65 HP.

Stage 2: Sourcing The Supply Of Water For The Water Bottling Plant
When treated with reverse osmosis, water generates residual water to the tune of 30% of the total volume. It takes approximately 1.39 litres of water to produce a litre of bottled water (as per IBWA). Therefore sourcing a clean and constant supply of water is must. As CGWA NOC will not be granted for abstracting groundwater in a notified area for bottling plants; therefore, the plant has to use sources like natural springs, ponds, mountain and hill water runoffs, etc. After identifying the source of the plant, it is advisable to construct storage for water collection.

Stage 3: Sourcing Raw Material For Water Bottling Plant
Water and plastic used to make bottles used in packaging are the two raw materials required. While PET plastic is the most commonly used plastic, some bottled water companies use polycarbonate plastic for their bottles. These are large-capacity bottles that can be washed and refilled again and again. Some premium brands are using glass bottles, too, for bottling drinking water.

Stage 4: Determining The Expense Of Running A Water Bottling Plant
Ongoing expenses include electricity and other consumable expenses for running a water bottling plant. Water purification is energy intensive due to the power involved in reverse osmosis and other treatments (UV radiation, ozone treatment, etc.).The cost of the filter and maintenance on the plant labour cost are all factors that must be considered when planning for the business. Utility costs can also be high due to any other ultrahigh purifying and value addition to the product. Treatment of RO discharge water may also add to the cost.

Stage 5: Obtain Necessary Licences And Permits For Water Bottling Plant
The following licenses and approvals are to be obtained for setting up a water bottling plant in India.

Business Registration: Registering a bottling business as a legal business entity will provide you with specific incentives and assistance announced by the government and establish a separate legal identity for your business.

ISI certification:  it is compulsory for all manufacturers who intend to set up processing units to obtain the ISI certification from the Bureau of India Standards (BIS). Packaged Mineral Water is regulated under IS:13428, and Packaged Drinking Water is governed under IS:14543.

Consent NOC

Every Water Bottling Plant needs a pollution NOC from the State Pollution Control Board. The NOC is granted under the green category as the bottling plant does not generate any significant waste apart from RO water discard. Consent NOCs, which the concerned State Pollution Control Board or the Pollution Control Committee issues, are in 2 forms, i.e. Consent to Establish or CTE and Consent to Operate or CTO. Documents required for Consent NOC are

💧Duly filled Application Form of concerned SPCB.
💧Site or layout plan with a roadmap
💧Detailed Project Report.
💧Details of the manufacturing process and finished products
💧Details of installed waste management measures and pollution control measures
💧list of machinery, the capital cost of the unit (land, building, and plant machinery)
💧water balance, source of water, and its required quantity
💧Land documents such as rent /lease agreement
💧Industry Registration Documents.
💧Consent fee (as applicable)
💧Balanced Sheet Certified by a CA

Water test report from an authorised laboratory of raw water
💧Pest control certification
💧Test reports from SPCB-certified labs (chemist, microbiologists etc.)
💧Documents related to ownership of land/lease for setting up the plant
💧MOA, if applicable/partnership deed
💧Electrical load sanction
💧Sanction layout plan
💧Any additional document mentioned in the application form

FSSAI Licence for Water Bottling Plant

A water bottling plant must also get trade and food business licenses from FSSAI. Documentations required for this licence are

💧Photo ID
💧List of proposed food articles
💧A duly filled Form A or B by the applicant (as per the type of FSSAI Licence).
💧Partnership Deed/ Certificate of Incorporation, Memorandum of Association(MOA)/ Articles of Association (AOA)
💧Lease or rental agreement to prove land ownership.
FSMS plan
💧Supporting dossiers (NOC of Panchayat/ Municipality, health NOC)
💧List of raw materials suppliers
💧Form IX
💧Water test report through a certified NABL lab.
💧Import-export code (aka IEC) granted by DGFT
💧List of machines and equipment
💧List of directors and Partners list
💧Medical certificates for workers

EPR Authorisation for Water Bottling Plant

EPR compliance is mandatory for all the water bottling plants as it is introducing plastic packaging with its product. The water bottling plant is required to obtain EPR Registration from CPCB or SPCBs/PCCs depending on the extent of their operation. Documents needed to obtain EPR Authorisation are

💧DIC Certificate
💧Consent NOC under Air & Water Act[1],
💧EPR Action Plan for fulfilling EPR liability (State/UT wise)
💧Document issued by ULB / designated state authority related to engagement of PIBO/WMA (waste management authority)
💧Registration issued by SPCBs/PCCs to Plastic waste 💧processing facility (PWPF) that will be engaged with for plastic waste processing
💧Agreement of water bottling plant with PWPF for PW processing
💧Agreement of plant with WMA

Miscellaneous stages in setting up the Water Bottling Plant

💧Defining your Bottled Water Business Brand
💧Creating your Bottled Water Business Website to create an online presence

Conclusion
Consumers prefer bottled water because it is easily available, easy to carry, store and transport. The bottling business profit margin is also adequate, and one can start this business with a moderate investment. Water and power costs will determine the running cost of a water bottling plant. Therefore a smart investment today will be to switch to renewable energy sources apart from other popular business practices that can be learnt from the assistance of industry experts. Also, licencing and authorisation processes can be streamlined and fast-forwarded with proper guidance.

In a major relief for homebuyers, the Bombay High Court has ruled that builders cannot avoid complying with RERA appellate orders by forcing buyers into fresh legal proceedings. The court said the MahaRERA Appellate Tribunal has the authority to enforce its own orders and, if necessary, appoint another agency to complete a stalled housing project at the defaulting builder's cost. The ruling came in a case involving homebuyers of Shri Vallabh Residency and is expected to strengthen the rights of thousands of homebuyers facing delayed projects.

#Punetimesmirror #BombayHighCourt #RERA #Homebuyers #RealEstate

@ThePuneMirror @grok
Explain the precedence..

If the RMC plants start emitting disproportionate pollution

Will the SCI still privilege the right to home in the scheduled time

Or

Stop the RMC operations until it meets the norms

Both the right to shelter (Chameli Singh, 1996) and right to clean environment (MC Mehta cases, Vellore Citizens) flow from Article 21. Supreme Court applies sustainable development + precautionary principle: environment prevails when norms are breached.

RMC plants require environmental clearance + SPCB consent. Disproportionate pollution triggers immediate regulatory/NGT/SC action to stop or strictly regulate operations until compliant — seen in Delhi GRAP stages and NGT cases against illegal batching plants. Project timelines under RERA (or the recent Bombay HC Shri Vallabh ruling enforcing tribunal orders) do not override pollution laws.

SCI would direct compliance first, allowing completion only through lawful means. No automatic privilege for “scheduled time” over clean air.
