ESG.text:
India🇮🇳 has become the world’s largest hub for retail Global Capability Centres, with 180 centres operating in the country, according to a new report. These GCCs have shifted from traditional back office support to acting as digital nerve centres for global retailers, running functions across supply chain, payments, fulfilment, customer analytics, merchandising and AI operations. Nearly 70 percent of retail GCCs in India now operate as multi functional control towers, connecting end to end operations for global brands. Major companies like Tesco, Adidas, Target, Wayfair, Lululemon and Hy-Vee have set up centres, with Tesco’s Bengaluru hub handling CX design, supply chain digitisation and ESG aligned retail operations, and Target’s Bengaluru GCC developing Store Companion, a GenAI chatbot now used in more than 2,000 US stores.

The report notes that while Bengaluru and Hyderabad remain the main hubs, retail and CPG companies are adopting a multi city strategy, with new players choosing Pune, Hyderabad, Mumbai and Chennai. India overall hosts over 2,100 GCCs employing 2.36 million people and generating close to 100 billion dollars in revenue. The retail segment alone has more than 70 GCCs employing over 85,000 people, a figure expected to grow significantly in the next three years. India’s AI native talent pool of 120,000 AI/ML professionals and more than 185 AI centres of excellence has been a key draw, enabling firms to move experimentation into production. New roles emerging include GenAI product leads, responsible AI and compliance architects, global ESG reporting specialists and omnichannel personalisation experts. Executives say the success of these centres has changed how global firms view India, turning it from an afterthought into a top priority market for investment and local leadership.

- World Business News.

#Exclusive: @sauraveswarsen , founder of @catlearnserv  Catalyst Learning Services, is helping young Indians build careers in public policy, sustainability, ESG, CSR and social entrepreneurship. Established in 2011, the organisation addresses the lack of structured guidance for social impact careers by offering mentorship, experiential learning and career support. Having mentored over 1,400 aspirants nearly 90% women Catalyst is preparing a new generation of professionals committed to solving pressing social and environmental challenges.

👉🏻 Building Careers With Purpose

After leaving his engineering career at 23, Sen founded Catalyst Learning Services to bridge the gap in career guidance for humanities and social science students. Through mentoring, field immersions and collaborations with universities, governments and NGOs, the organisation equips learners with practical skills for careers in governance, climate action, development and sustainability. Many alumni now work with organisations such as the UN, UNICEF and WHO.

Preparing Future Changemakers

Catalyst has expanded its impact beyond student mentoring by training over 800 teachers and supporting social enterprises in mental healthcare, renewable energy, waste management and livelihoods. As emerging fields like AI, ESG and climate resilience reshape the future of work, the organisation continues to evolve its programmes, helping students develop empathy, leadership and systems thinking alongside professional expertise.

The Logical Indian's Perspective

Sauraveswar Sen's journey demonstrates that education can be a powerful catalyst for social transformation when it empowers people to serve communities, not just secure jobs. As India navigates complex challenges like climate change and inequality, purpose-driven education can shape a more inclusive future.

#SocialImpact #Education #Sustainability #PublicPolicy #YouthEmpowerment #TheLogicalIndian

Indian markets have a new metric for evaluating stocks: violence.   

NSE just launched an index that picks companies on “Ahimsa” (non-violence) called the Nifty500 Ahimsa. This follows the launch of BSE's Saatvik 100 index last month which tracks "pure, value-conscious" companies.

Standard ESG indices (like NIFTY100 ESG) rank companies on their overall impact on the world using a broad scope and multiple parameters. NSE's Ahimsa zooms in on whether a company is "himsak"/ violent towards humans or animals. They seem to have a zero-tolerance policy towards anything remotely meat-adjacent - airlines and hotels that serve meat, as well as the banks that lend to them are excluded. Inclusion methodology in the picture below - only "green" stocks go in.

Offering people a chance to invest in their personalised vision for the world seems to now be a thing. Maybe ESG doesn't cut if for you and your ideal world is one where nobody serves or eats chicken - you now have an index. Maybe, in time, we'll see an index of companies whose CEOs meditate before work.

♻️ INDIA'S RECYCLING ECONOMY | A Multi-Decade Investment Theme? 🇮🇳

India is moving rapidly towards a circular economy, where waste is no longer discarded but converted into valuable raw materials. Rising commodity prices, stricter environmental regulations, urbanisation, EV adoption, and government initiatives are creating long-term opportunities across the recycling ecosystem.

Instead of viewing recycling as a traditional business, investors are increasingly tracking it as an emerging structural growth theme.

🔋 Lead Recycling

🔹 Gravita India – One of India's largest lead recyclers with a growing presence in aluminium, plastic and rubber recycling.

🔹 Pondy Oxides & Chemicals – Major player in lead recycling and lead alloy manufacturing.

🔹 Nile Ltd – Lead recycling and battery material business catering to industrial demand.

---

♻️ Metal Recycling

🔹 Nupur Recyclers – Processes ferrous and non-ferrous scrap for industrial applications.

🔹 Jain Resource Recycling – Focused on recovering valuable metals from industrial waste.

🔹 Baheti Recycling Industries – Aluminium recycling benefiting from increasing demand for lightweight metals.

---

🗑️ Waste Management & Municipal Services

🔹 MSTC – Government-backed e-auction platform facilitating scrap disposal and resource recovery.

🔹 Antony Waste Handling Cell – Integrated municipal solid waste (MSW) collection, processing and landfill management.

🔹 A2Z Infra Engineering – Waste collection, transportation and municipal waste processing.

🔹 Urban Enviro Waste Management – Solid waste management services across multiple cities.

---

💧 Water Recycling & Sewage Treatment

🔹 VA Tech Wabag – Global leader in water treatment, desalination and wastewater recycling.

🔹 EMS Ltd – Sewage treatment plants (STPs), water supply and wastewater infrastructure projects.

🔹 Felix Industries – Industrial wastewater treatment and water recycling solutions.

---

🛞 Tyre & Rubber Recycling

🔹 GRP Ltd – Recycled rubber products with domestic and export markets.

🔹 Elgi Rubber Company – Tyre retreading and rubber recycling solutions.

🔹 Tinna Rubber & Infrastructure – Converts waste tyres into crumb rubber and modified bitumen.

---

🧴 Plastic & PET Recycling

🔹 Ganesha Ecosphere – India's leading PET bottle recycling company producing recycled polyester fibre.

🔹 Race Eco Chain – Plastic waste collection and recycling with a focus on sustainability.

---

💻 E-Waste Recycling

🔹 Cerebra Integrated Technologies – IT asset management and electronic waste recycling.

🔹 Eco Recycling (Ecoreco) – One of India's oldest organised e-waste recyclers.

🔹 Namo eWaste Management – End-to-end e-waste collection, processing and recycling.

📈 Why This Theme Could Grow Over the Next Decade

✅ India's circular economy policy push
✅ Rapid increase in electronic waste generation
✅ Higher recycling targets across industries
✅ Growing demand for recycled metals and plastics
✅ Rising battery recycling opportunity from EV adoption
✅ Water scarcity driving wastewater recycling projects
✅ ESG investing 

📌 Save this list for your watchlist.

🇮🇳 TATA STEEL AGM / INVESTOR PRESENTATION | 02 JULY 2026 🚨

🚨 Tata Steel's biggest message wasn't the FY26 earnings—it was the next phase of growth. Management is targeting over 40 MTPA steel capacity in India, accelerating downstream expansion, embedding AI across operations, maintaining financial discipline, and staying committed to Net Zero by 2045 while delivering record profitability.

📊 10 Biggest Takeaways
📌 1. Record FY26 Financial Performance

Tata Steel reported one of its strongest financial years:

Revenue: ₹2,32,140 Cr (+6.2% YoY)
EBITDA: ₹34,848 Cr (+35% YoY)
PAT: ₹10,886 Cr (3.4x YoY)
Steel Deliveries: 31.97 Million Tonnes
EPS: ₹8.7
Dividend: ₹4/share.
📌 2. India Capacity Expansion Accelerating

Management reiterated plans to expand Indian crude steel capacity:

Current Capacity: ~27.4 MTPA
Target: 40+ MTPA

Major projects include:

4.8 MTPA expansion at NINL
2.5 MTPA finished steel expansion at Meramandali
6 MTPA Greenfield Maharashtra plant

This represents one of India's largest ongoing steel expansion programs.

📌 3. Downstream Business Becoming Bigger

Investments continue across:

Tubes (1.7 → 4 MTPA)
Tinplate (0.4 → 1 MTPA)
Wires
Colour Coated Steel
Cold Rolled Steel
0.7 MTPA HRPGL Complex

Management continues shifting towards higher-value downstream products.

📌 4. ₹10,868 Cr Cost Savings Delivered

FY26 Cost Transformation Program delivered:

₹10,868 Cr savings
95% of targeted savings achieved

Savings came from:

Raw material optimization
Supply chain improvements
Stores & maintenance
Power & fuel efficiency
UK restructuring initiatives

FY27 cost-saving target remains ₹7,140 Cr.

📌 5. AI Becoming Core to Manufacturing

Management highlighted increasing AI deployment across operations:

Fuel optimization
Energy efficiency
Process automation
Predictive maintenance
Safety surveillance
Customer platforms

Digital platforms generated US$1 Billion Gross Merchandise Value during FY26, demonstrating increasing digital adoption.

📌 6. Sustainability Remains a Strategic Priority

Key announcements:

Net Zero target remains 2045
UK decarbonization expected to reduce 50 million tonnes of CO₂e over the decade.
€300+ million invested in sustainability-linked initiatives.
Multiple decarbonization projects underway in India.
📌 7. Financial Discipline Continues

Management highlighted:

₹14,026 Cr FY26 Capex
Approximately ₹23,000 Cr dividends paid over the last 5 years
Consolidated EBITDA margin improved by approximately 320 basis points YoY, despite global steel market headwinds.
Continued focus on capital allocation and balance-sheet optimization.
📌 8. Safety & Workforce Investments Continue

Highlights include:

AI-enabled workplace safety
Six Safety Leadership Development Centers
560+ health sessions
1,000+ wellness champions
Lost Time Injury Frequency Rate reduced by 38% over the last 15 years.
📌 9. Innovation Pipeline Remains Strong

Key milestones:

550+ new steel products launched in the last five years.
100+ patents filed annually.
World's first industrial deployment of EASyMelt technology.
1 MTPA HIsarna demonstration progressing.
Over 90% of Indian production now comes from certified sites.
📌 10. ESG & Community Impact

Management reported:

69 lakh+ lives impacted through CSR.
₹2,500+ Cr CSR spend over the past five years.
38,000+ households adopting climate-resilient agriculture.
20,000+ persons with disabilities supported through the SABAL programme.
Recognition as worldsteel Sustainability Champion for the 9th consecutive year.
🎯 What Investors Should Watch
✅ Positives
Strong earnings recovery.
India expansion from 27.4 → 40+ MTPA.
Significant cost transformation program.
AI-led productivity gains.
Strong downstream steel strategy.
Healthy capital allocation with sustained dividends.
Continued leadership in sustainability and technology.
⚠️ Key Risks
Global steel price volatility.
Raw material cost fluctuations.
Timely execution of large-scale expansion projects.
European demand and profitability during the decarbonization transition.
📌 Bottom Line

Tata Steel is positioning itself for the next decade by combining scale expansion, higher-value downstream products, AI-driven manufacturing, disciplined cost control, and decarbonization. With FY26 revenue of ₹2.32 lakh crore, EBITDA of ₹34,848 crore, PAT of ₹10,886 crore, over ₹10,800 crore of cost savings, and a roadmap to exceed 40 MTPA capacity in India, management is focused on delivering sustainable growth while strengthening its competitive position across global steel markets

Bank of Maharashtra has presented its financial results for the quarter ended June 30, 2026 (Q1 FY27), showcasing robust growth and improved asset quality.

𝗞𝗲𝘆 𝗙𝗶𝗻𝗮𝗻𝗰𝗶𝗮𝗹 𝗛𝗶𝗴𝗵𝗹𝗶𝗴𝗵𝘁𝘀:

- 𝗡𝗲𝘁 𝗣𝗿𝗼𝗳𝗶𝘁: Increased by a significant 26.84% 𝗬𝗼𝗬 to ₹2,020 Crore.
- 𝗢𝗽𝗲𝗿𝗮𝘁𝗶𝗻𝗴 𝗣𝗿𝗼𝗳𝗶𝘁: Saw a 21.29% 𝗬𝗼𝗬 growth.
- 𝗡𝗲𝘁 𝗜𝗻𝘁𝗲𝗿𝗲𝘀𝘁 𝗜𝗻𝗰𝗼𝗺𝗲 (𝗡𝗜𝗜): Rose by 14.53% 𝗬𝗼𝗬.
- 𝗧𝗼𝘁𝗮𝗹 𝗕𝘂𝘀𝗶𝗻𝗲𝘀𝘀: Grew by 19.10% 𝗬𝗼𝗬 to ₹6,50,457 Crore.
- 𝗚𝗿𝗼𝘀𝘀 𝗔𝗱𝘃𝗮𝗻𝗰𝗲𝘀: Experienced a substantial 26.90% 𝗬𝗼𝗬 increase, reaching ₹3,05,964 Crore.
- 𝗧𝗼𝘁𝗮𝗹 𝗗𝗲𝗽𝗼𝘀𝗶𝘁𝘀: Increased by 12.93% 𝗬𝗼𝗬 to ₹3,44,493 Crore.
- 𝗥𝗲𝘁𝘂𝗿𝗻 𝗼𝗻 𝗔𝘀𝘀𝗲𝘁𝘀 (𝗥𝗢𝗔): Improved to 1.90% (from 1.80% YoY).
- 𝗥𝗲𝘁𝘂𝗿𝗻 𝗼𝗻 𝗘𝗾𝘂𝗶𝘁𝘆 (𝗥𝗢𝗘): Stood at 24.65%.

𝗕𝘂𝘀𝗶𝗻𝗲𝘀𝘀 𝗣𝗲𝗿𝗳𝗼𝗿𝗺𝗮𝗻𝗰𝗲:

- 𝗧𝗼𝘁𝗮𝗹 𝗗𝗲𝗽𝗼𝘀𝗶𝘁𝘀: Grew to ₹3,44,493 Cr, with Savings Deposits up 15.61% YoY and Term Deposits up 16.62% YoY.
- 𝗚𝗹𝗼𝗯𝗮𝗹 𝗔𝗱𝘃𝗮𝗻𝗰𝗲𝘀: Reached ₹3,05,964 Cr, with strong growth in Retail Advances (+24.59% YoY), Agri Advances (+28.94% YoY), and MSME Advances (+23.17% YoY).
- 𝗖𝗔𝗦𝗔 𝗥𝗮𝘁𝗶𝗼: Remained strong at 49%.

𝗔𝘀𝘀𝗲𝘁 𝗤𝘂𝗮𝗹𝗶𝘁𝘆 𝗜𝗺𝗽𝗿𝗼𝘃𝗲𝗺𝗲𝗻𝘁:

- 𝗚𝗿𝗼𝘀𝘀 𝗡𝗣𝗔 𝗥𝗮𝘁𝗶𝗼: Declined to 1.45% (from 1.74% YoY).
- 𝗡𝗲𝘁 𝗡𝗣𝗔 𝗥𝗮𝘁𝗶𝗼: Improved to 0.13% (from 0.18% YoY).
- 𝗣𝗿𝗼𝘃𝗶𝘀𝗶𝗼𝗻 𝗖𝗼𝘃𝗲𝗿𝗮𝗴𝗲 𝗥𝗮𝘁𝗶𝗼 (𝗣𝗖𝗥): Increased to 98.55% (from 98.36% YoY).

𝗖𝗮𝗽𝗶𝘁𝗮𝗹 𝗔𝗱𝗲𝗾𝘂𝗮𝗰𝘆:

ESG.text:
Chanakya Sutrani: 1  

True prosperity cannot be built on a foundation of injustice. If an individual or a state seeks happiness through unethical means, that happiness will be unstable and short-lived. 

Therefore, Chanakya places "Dharma" at the very beginning as the essential prerequisite for a successful life. 

 सुख का मूल धर्म है। (अर्थात: नैतिक कर्तव्यों का पालन करना ही सुख का आधार है।)

Chanakya, ancient Indian philosopher and strategist behind the Arthashastra, taught these principles to guide both personal conduct and state governance toward enduring stability rather than fleeting gains.

In today's competitive economies and politics, the sutra warns that pursuing wealth or power through #corruption or shortcuts often leads to scandals, legal repercussions, and reputational collapse, echoing corporate failures like Enron or recent governance crises.

It supports modern psychology and well-being research showing that ethical living and purpose-driven actions correlate with higher long-term life satisfaction and mental health, unlike fleeting gains from unethical behavior.

For leaders and individuals, it promotes sustainable success through integrity, trust-building, and ethical decision-making—principles central to ESG investing, corporate governance standards, and personal development frameworks emphasizing character over quick wins.

Thank you for acknowledging our sustainability efforts. This should address your concerns: 

1. Our international renewable energy certificates (IREC) for Scope 2 emissions, and carbon offset purchases for Scope 1 and Scope 3 emissions have been verified by our ESG assurance providers for three years in a row. These assurance letters are available in the public domain
• ESG Factsheet For FY24 (Page 36) - https://t.co/MgT0VN6U7G
• ESG Factsheet for FY23 (Page 22) - https://t.co/uehdIPZMH5
• ESG Update for FY22 (Page 47) - https://t.co/IAZTFkLDec

You will note that IREC and offset purchases equal Zomato's entire carbon footprint for its food delivery business for three years in a row making our deliveries 100% carbon neutral.

2. Since the launch of plastic neutral deliveries in FY 23, we have certificates from our authorized waste management agency and recycling partner IPCA for 30,000MT of plastic waste recycled. This amount is  ~ 1.5x the volume of plastic waste generated by orders delivered through Zomato as per a detailed estimate conducted by an independent third party (one of the big four). Since our restaurant partners choose their own packaging, this is a completely voluntary initiative on our part to mitigate plastic pollution. Having said that, we run multiple programs to encourage restaurant partners to shift towards sustainable packaging options.

3. We pledged to achieve 100 per cent EV-based deliveries by 2030 way back in 2021. We believe this effort will significantly contribute to our commitment to Net Zero by 2033 for the food delivery business. Close to 1 in 3 deliveries in Delhi are already being done on EVs.

4. On a separate note - today's 2W EV products, charging infrastructure and financing facilities are yet to evolve sufficiently to meet the specific requirements and constraints of our food delivery partners at scale and we are actively working with policymakers, industry bodies and the ecosystem of EV players pan-India to solve for this by our stated timeline.

Our efforts to support the adoption of EVs by our delivery partners are covered in our latest Business Responsibility and Sustainability Report (on Page 107) of our Annual Report (https://t.co/YKwaTPMkjl), which again is available in the public domain.

Administrators have begun marketing KOKO Networks' assets, seeking qualified buyers capable of completing transactions exceeding $15 million, with expressions of interest due by July 17. 

The sale covers patents, hardware designs, software, a stove and canister manufacturing plant in India, and a distribution platform that supported more than 3,000 automated fuel stations in Kenya. 

KOKO shut down in January and laid off more than 700 employees after the government declined to issue a Letter of Authorisation required to sell carbon credits internationally, cutting off revenues used to subsidise ethanol fuel for more than one million Kenyan households. 

PwC is administering KOKO Networks Limited, while affiliated Indian companies are undergoing voluntary liquidation. The company was backed by investors including Microsoft's Climate Innovation Fund, Mirova, Verod-Kepple Africa Ventures and Rand Merchant Bank.

TERI, VNV, and KCC Join Hands for Agroforestry-Based Carbon Finance Project in Haryana

TERI, the Kisan Chamber of Commerce (KCC), and Value Network Ventures (@vnvadvisory) signed a tripartite Memorandum of Understanding (MoU) at the Constitution Club of India, New Delhi, to collaborate on the development and implementation of an agroforestry-based carbon finance project in Haryana.

The MoU was signed by Dr JV Sharma, Senior Director, Land Resources, TERI; Mr Sandeep Roy Choudhury, Director, VNV; and Mr Suresh Deshwal, General Secretary, KCC, in the presence of representatives from the partner organisations.

The partnership aims to promote climate-smart agriculture and sustainable land management by enabling farmers to participate in emerging carbon markets. Through improved agroforestry and agricultural practices, the initiative seeks to support carbon sequestration, generate carbon credits, and create additional income opportunities for farmers.

For the Indian Venture and Alternate Capital Association (@IndianVCA) Clean Air Forum 2026, IVCA assessed the Forum’s carbon footprint in partnership with EnviroWealth, covering delegate travel, on-site electricity, catering and materials.

The assessment estimated a total footprint of 7.16 tCO2e for the 160-delegate convening, which was fully offset through UNFCCC-verified renewable-energy carbon credits, making the Forum a carbon neutral event.

IVCA also continued its speaker-recognition tree-planting initiative in collaboration with @GBT_global. Together with the wider plantation associated with the Forum, 45 geotagged guava trees were planted, with estimated annual sequestration of 990 kg of CO2.

The Forum also incorporated lower-carbon choices including a 100% vegetarian menu, zero single-use plastic, no plastic water bottles, paperless content delivery and electric induction cooking.

As IVCA continues to convene conversations on climate, capital and clean air, the objective is to keep strengthening how these platforms are designed, measured and delivered.

Carbon Credits.text:
EKI Energy is the one clear listed company in India whose primary business is carbon-credit development/sales and climate advisory.

India has committed to net-zero emissions by 2070. That means industries will either cut emissions or buy carbon credits to offset them. This creates a multi-billion-dollar compliance and voluntary market.

In 2023, the Indian govt launched the CCTS (Carbon Credit Trading Scheme). This sets the framework for an official carbon market, where credits will be traded like commodities (similar to power exchanges).

Global Push & Exports
Europe’s CBAM (Carbon Border Adjustment Mechanism) will penalize carbon-intensive imports from 2026. Indian exporters will need carbon credits to stay competitive.

🌍 Why Mukesh Ambani is Interested

1. Reliance Net-Zero 2035 Target
Ambani announced Reliance will achieve net-zero by 2035 — much earlier than India’s 2070 target. Carbon credits are a critical lever to get there.

2. Massive Renewable Push
Reliance is investing ₹75,000+ crore into green hydrogen, solar PV, batteries. Each project can generate carbon credits (RECs, offsets).

3. Carbon Credit as a New Business
Ambani sees carbon credits not just as a compliance tool, but as a trading business (like oil trading in the past). Owning carbon-credit generating assets + trading platform could make Reliance a leader.

4. Global Opportunity
The voluntary carbon market is projected to grow from ~$2 billion now to $250+ billion by 2050. Reliance, with its global scale, can capture export-linked opportunities

India’s Data Centre Boom: A Decade of Exponential Growth 🚀

India’s data centre capacity is set for a massive expansion over the next decade, driven by AI, cloud computing, and 5G adoption.

🔹 Key Highlights
- Current capacity: ~1.5 GW
- Projected capacity by 2035: ~14 GW
- Growth rate: ~20% CAGR
- Estimated investments: USD 70 billion by FY35

🔹 Market Landscape
- Data centres classified into hyperscalers (AI & cloud), co-location facilities, and edge data centres (focused on low latency in Tier-II cities).
- Over 90% of capacity concentrated in Mumbai, Chennai, Delhi-NCR, Bengaluru, Pune, Hyderabad, and Kolkata due to power availability and enterprise proximity.

🔹 Tax & Regulatory Challenges
1. Direct Tax Issues
- Ambiguity in depreciation rates for integrated assets (buildings, servers, cooling systems).
- Income characterisation risks for cloud services.
- Unclear deductibility of carbon credits for net-zero goals.
- PE/SEP risks for foreign players due to low attribution thresholds.

2. Indirect Tax Issues
- GST input tax credit blocked on construction (except plant & machinery).
- Leasehold premiums taxed upfront, leading to ITC disputes.
- SEZ compliance inspections disrupting high-security operations.

🔹 The Way Forward
- Short term: Clear tax policies on PE/SEP, depreciation, and GST credits.
- Medium term: Green incentives, faster depreciation for energy-efficient designs, data localisation tax benefits.
- Long term: Development of data embassies and integrated data cities with tax-neutral sovereign zones.

📌 Bottom line: 
With supportive policies and tax clarity, India is well-positioned to emerge as a trusted global data hub in the coming decade.

T890-            Delhi Metro 🚈 

The Delhi Metro stands out as one of India's most efficient and extensive urban rail networks, serving not just Delhi but also extending into neighboring cities like Noida, Gurgaon, Faridabad, Ghaziabad, Bahadurgarh, and Ballabhgarh within the National Capital Region (NCR). Here's a comprehensive overview based on the latest information up to October 2024:

- Network and Expansion: As of February 2020, the Delhi Metro network comprised 10 color-coded lines with 256 stations, covering a total length of 350.42 kilometers. This makes it one of the largest metro systems by length in India. The system continues to expand, with Phase IV projects under construction aiming to extend the network further.

- Operational Features: The metro operates from around 05:00 to 00:00, with trains running at peak frequencies of 2-3 minutes during busy hours. It's noted for its cleanliness, modern communication systems, and air-conditioned coaches, making it a preferred mode of transport for millions daily.

- Environmental Impact: The Delhi Metro Rail Corporation (DMRC) was recognized by the United Nations in 2011 for being the first metro system globally to receive carbon credits for reducing greenhouse gas emissions, underscoring its commitment to sustainability.

- Cultural Integration: Stations like INA Colony feature art and handicrafts, and sections like the Central Secretariat to Qutub Minar on the Yellow Line showcase Delhi's architectural heritage, blending utility with cultural representation.

- Recent Developments and Public Sentiment: Recent posts on X (formerly Twitter) reflect mixed feelings about the metro's current state. While some users applaud its cleanliness and efficiency compared to other global metro systems like New York's, others express concerns over increased crowding, delays, and maintenance issues. For instance, there was mention of an increase in train trips to manage crowds better, indicating ongoing efforts to enhance service quality.

- Infrastructure Details: The metro includes both elevated and underground sections, with notable stations like Hauz Khas being the deepest at 29 meters below ground, and the Pink and Orange Lines crossing at Dhaula Kuan being the highest point. This infrastructure diversity speaks to the engineering challenges and solutions implemented across varied urban landscapes.

- Connectivity: The Delhi Metro not only serves as a vital lifeline within Delhi but also connects to regional bus and rail networks, enhancing its role in the broader public transport ecosystem of the NCR.

- Future Prospects: With ongoing and planned expansions, the Delhi Metro aims to further reduce road congestion, lower air pollution, and improve connectivity across the region, aligning with urban development goals of making transport efficient and sustainable.

The Delhi Metro, thus, remains a critical component of Delhi's infrastructure, praised for its efficiency, yet facing the common urban transit challenges of maintaining service quality amidst growing demand and infrastructure wear.

Bengaluru-based entrepreneurs Abhimanyu Rathi and Yogendra Panchal have solved one of India's biggest problems.

Indian farmers used to burn their crop leftovers after every harvest. Now a Bengaluru startup is paying farmers not to burn it.

The residue is heated in low-oxygen conditions and turns into biochar, a carbon-rich material that locks carbon underground instead of releasing it into the air.

This climate benefit is sold as carbon credits to companies. Farmers can earn Rs 30,000–45,000 extra every year from waste they used to throw away.

Over 200 farmers are already in the system. This can be a game changer for Delhi pollution.

How Govt and Industry in Bihar can take advantage of the Indo - Japan Vision for the Next Decade?

1. Build a roadmap against each of the strategic priorities agreed between India & Japan
 
2. Plan and build capacity to train 5,000 skilled and semi-skilled personnel from Bihar as part of the 50,000 target from India. Bihari skills with mechanical engines is well known. If the govt and industry focus on few engineering colleges and few ITIs as excellence targets, this can easily be done.

3. Explore how Bihar’s rural lifestyle can be leveraged towards decarbonizing Crediting Mechanism. A great advantage of high rural lifestyle is their low carbon footprint and environmentally sustainable living. Building mechanisms to turn this into carbon credits can yield significant value both for India as well as for the rural communities
 
4. Bihar is just starting on its Digital everywhere journey. Partnering with Japan towards IoT and sensor networks monitoring public infrastructure - roads, bridges, hospitals, ports etc and using Japan’s prowess in semiconductors, electronics to setup capacity at technical institutes will be a great outcome
 
5. As Sanjay ji’s opinion expresses, there are already strong bonds on Buddhist circuit. The buddhist corridor can be further strengthened by including other locations with historical relationships with Buddhist heritage (Ma Tara at Mahishi). A mithila art museum at Madhubani with collaboration from Hasegawa’s museum at Tokamachi would be a major attraction bringing tourists into Madhubani.
 
6. The Indo-Japan MoU visualised work on Domestic Waste Water Management. This is a critical area as Bihar looks at creating 10 new satellite townships and plans urban expansion.
 
7. Low urbanisation has meant that much of Bihar’s forest and environment are still not impacted by the pace of development. The Indo-Japan MOU on cooperation in the field of environment can provide umbrella cover to advise the state as its moves towards urbanisation.

8. Bihar has been a cradle of textile development since centuries. The finest silk, linen were produced here. Finding markets in Japan for fabrics and products would add to the economic development of the state.
 
9. Bihar govt has announced an AI city recently. An MOU with a leading Japanese city and co-development of the AI city in collaboration with Japan could open up this domain for Bihar and bring much needed private investment into Bihar. The Indo - Japan AI initiative also plays into this.

10. Bihar has turned around its roads and now road transportation to all parts is easy, convenient and smooth. It seeks to now build a modern, EV powered bus service across the state. Partnering with Japanese companies towards this can be a big win-win under the Indo-Japan MOU.
 
11. Bihar’s SMEs are looking to upgrade their technologies, machinery and quality systems. The India - Japan Small and Medium Enterprises Forum to strengthen collaboration between Indian and Japanese SMEs can be used to find opportunities for Bihar SMEs through BIA, CII and other industry forums.
 
12. The Bihar diaspora is quite active in Japan and has been working on cultural events. Strengthening their initiatives and building business forums between India and the two regions of Kansai and Kyushu to strengthen business, people to people and cultural linkages can be implemented through the association.

End of Life Vehicle (ELV) Credits are another one of @dovuofficial's innovative sustainable offerings on their platform. 

Individuals and Organizations can purchase them to offset their Carbon footprint. They provide a tangible way to demonstrate commitment to sustainability through responsible ELV recycling. ♻️

Currently live on https://t.co/LSV7bsREPu is the Sustainable End-of-Life Vehicle Scrapping Program supported by the Government of India. 🇮🇳

Enhancing market transparency and traceability, an ELV Credit represents the environmental impact of processing an End-of-Life Vehicle (ELV) through a Registered Vehicle Scrapping Facility (RVSFs), which is authorised by the Government. 

Each Credit is uniquely linked to an individual ELV backed by multiple traceability data points. This, in turn, enables consumers to see the direct sustainable environmental impact of their purchase.🥬 

The carbon emission avoidance values vary depending on the specific ELVs characteristics and recycling outcomes. 

‘Dovu have built their platform to be the infrastructure of digital green credits at scale’.

India has close to 20 million vehicles approaching their end of life, with only 0.1% of those vehicles finding their way to official scrapping channels. 🚗

Unofficial scrapping facilities contribute to environmental harm and health risks by releasing hazardous substances into the land and atmosphere. ⚠️ 

The re-sale of unfit car parts also endangers vehicle safety and reliability, increasing the risk on roads to public health. 

With a Registered Vehicle Scrapping Facility (RVSFs), environmentally friendly scrapping is ensured, with correct disposal of harmful elements and waste management. 🚮

The Indian Government is promoting the establishment of RVSFs adhering to environmental standards. However, the costs of addressing compliance, managing ELV waste and handling hazardous materials and unfit components creates difficulties for businesses to compete in the market.

Meta Materials Circular Markets (MMCM) is addressing this issue by introducing carbon credits to the ELV market with their Sustainable End-of-Life Vehicle Scrapping Program supported by the Indian Government.

MMCM has been formed by two Indian giants (MTC Group and NeML) to accelerate the country’s race towards net zero with DOVU’s innovative technology at the foundation utilising the @hedera Guardian.

ELV Credits will create a new revenue stream for businesses, therefore making it financially viable to adhere to environmental standards and in turn encouraging sustainable practices within the ELV industry.

Minted on Dovu’s platform these credits offer a transparent registry that traces their journey from inception to retirement.

Sustainability.text:
Power sector alert from S&P Global ⚠️

India’s power distribution companies (discoms) are showing better earnings & liquidity — but this is largely masked by heavy government subsidies, grants & cash injections rather than deep structural reforms.

**Key warning:** Most discoms would post sizable losses without subsidies. Their finances remain closely tied to strained state budgets, raising long-term sustainability concerns.

S&P emphasizes that **structural reforms** — better efficiency, lower losses, reduced subsidy dependence, and privatisation successes (like in Odisha & Delhi) — are critical for a truly sustainable power sector.

Real progress needs more than temporary fixes.

For years, a carefully manufactured image was sold to the nation: 
Sonam Wangchuk as the real-life Rancho from 3 Idiots - the poor Ladakhi boy who rose from nothing, rejected the broken system, and became a revolutionary innovator fighting for the common man.

That story is a lie.

The Rancho character in 3 Idiots was never based on Sonam Wangchuk. It was actually the other way around; Wangchuk’s public image is based on the film. 
His entire public persona, the hero worship, and the “genius from the mountains” narrative were built on the back of *3 Idiots*. The movie turned him into a national icon. Without it, most of India wouldn’t even know his name.

The truth is far less cinematic.

Wangchuk was born into a politically influential family. His father, Sonam Wangyal, was a Congress MLA from Leh who later became a Cabinet Minister in the J&K government in 1975. When his father gained power, the family moved to Srinagar. This was not the life of a poor, downtrodden mountain boy struggling against all odds. This was access, connections, and political privilege from an early stage.

He did not study at an IIT. He completed his https://t.co/UBscbtn1Dh in Mechanical Engineering from the Regional Engineering College, Srinagar (now NIT Srinagar) in 1987, the same regional college that thousands of ordinary students attend. His admission there likely benefited from his father being a sitting Congress MLA and Minister at the time. Political family influence opened doors that truly poor students from remote areas rarely get.

He is not a scientist. He is a mechanical engineer by qualification who later became an environmental activist, often opposing development projects in Ladakh.

Despite all the hype around inventions like the Ice Stupa, zero patents are registered in his name. Not even one patent or one Research paper.

Yet this same privileged son of a Congress Minister is presented as the authentic voice of the voiceless poor. The same pattern is visible with others, for example, Abhijeet Dipke pursuing expensive liberal arts courses in fancy Boston colleges, or Saurav Das, the failed CLAT aspirant from rich, privileged South Delhi with zero real investment in on-ground public work. These are the people being pushed as the “representatives” of common, downtrodden Indians.

This is the real inconvenient truth:  

A section of the elite and politically connected class has mastered the art of wearing the mask of the poor and the marginalised. They build cults around themselves using films, media, and selective narratives while hiding their actual backgrounds of power, privilege, and influence.

Sonam Wangchuk’s story is not one of rags to revolutionary. It is a story of how political family privilege was repackaged as grassroots rebellion.

The myth is exposed. The facts don’t lie.

10 Things @Wangchuk66  did for India :

Here are verifiable contributions focused on education, environment, sustainability, and community resilience:

1.  Founded SECMOL (1988): Co-founded the Students’ Educational and Cultural Movement of Ladakh with peers. It promotes culturally relevant, practical, hands-on education over rote learning for Ladakhi students who struggled in the mainstream system.

2.  Led Operation New Hope (1994 onward): Collaborated with government, villages, and civil society to reform government schools in Ladakh. This dramatically improved matriculation pass rates (from very low levels) through local-language elements, better teaching, and community involvement. It influenced education policy. https://t.co/JyZvESIkIA 

3.  Built a model sustainable campus: Designed and runs the SECMOL alternative school campus—fully solar-powered, passive solar-heated mud/earth buildings that maintain livable indoor temperatures (e.g., +10–15°C) in extreme cold (-20°C or lower) with zero fossil fuels. Demonstrates low-cost, local-material eco-architecture. https://t.co/3sgsTzvewc 

4.  Invented/scaled Ice Stupas (2013–14 onward): Pioneered (building on earlier ideas) cone-shaped artificial glaciers using gravity-fed pipes to freeze winter stream water into tall ice towers. These melt slowly in spring/summer, providing millions of liters of irrigation water for farms facing glacier retreat and scarcity. Dozens built; replicated internationally (e.g., Alps). Won the Rolex Award for this. https://t.co/JyZvESIkIA 

5.  Promoted passive solar and earthen architecture: Designed affordable, climate-appropriate buildings using local mud/soil for communities, reducing energy needs in the Himalayas. Studied this formally in France and applied it widely. https://t.co/3sgsTzvewc 

6.  Co-founded Himalayan Institute of Alternatives, Ladakh (HIAL): An alternative higher-education initiative focused on mountain-specific issues like sustainability, tourism, and entrepreneurship. Uses practical, subsidized programs. https://t.co/HmGxdFRYBC 

7.  Developed solar innovations for the Indian Army: Created mobile solar-heated tents and eco-friendly structures for high-altitude use (e.g., Siachen/Galwan areas), helping troops in extreme cold (2021+). https://t.co/3sgsTzvewc 

8.  Advocated for Ladakh’s environment and culture: Led campaigns on climate change, glacier protection, sustainable tourism, and constitutional safeguards (e.g., 6th Schedule demands). Raised national awareness of Himalayan vulnerabilities, including post-2019 UT status issues. https://t.co/JyZvESIkIA 

9. His work highlights mountain India’s challenges and offers replicable models for arid/high-altitude areas across the country and globally.

Wangchuk’s record shows tangible, community-focused engineering and reform work in a fragile region—strengths in practical application rather than academic patents or elite credentials. Criticisms of his activism or project execution exist (e.g., local disputes), but his core contributions to education and water security in Ladakh are well-documented.

How do you power the future for 1.4 billion people? (it’s not what you think)

Imagine living in a country where the energy demand is growing so fast, it needs to build the equivalent of a brand-new power grid almost overnight.
That’s the reality for India right now.
As hundreds of millions of people rise into the middle class—buying their first appliances, cars, and air conditioners—the country needs to add a staggering 2,000 gigawatts of new power over the next 20 years.

How do we keep the lights on, keep electricity bills cheap, and save the planet all at the same time?
International leaders recently gathered at the iconic London Science Museum, alongside the Energy Transitions Commission, to solve this exact puzzle. It’s what Mr. Sagar Adani called the global trifecta:
Energy security
Energy affordability
Energy sustainability

This isn't just about utility bills—it's about the biggest energy transition in human history.
Want to see how the world's leading minds plan to conquer the global trifecta? Watch the exclusive event video of the  inaugural Adani Green Energy Dialogue 2026 – https://t.co/5QGX3xJjhN

7/ The leading indicator most will miss 📈

Watch the dossier funnel, not the quarterly P&L:

8/ The fine print ⚠️ (the catch from Tweet1)

• Valuation: the stock more than doubled in a year and trades well above its own historical range. Today's price assumes near flawless FY27 execution
• Margins: Q4's 36.9% EBITDA margin sits far above the company's own decade band of roughly 19% to 31%, and gross margin compressed from 71% to 60% in the same quarter. Sustainability unproven
• Working capital: inventory near 193 days and slowing collections. Cash conversion must improve as EU supplies scale
• Returns and dilution: ROE near 7%. Growth was equity funded, share count grew from 1.5 Cr to 2.2 Cr over five years
• Governance watch: secretarial auditor resigned mid-tenure in May 2026 citing personal reasons; NSE sought a results clarification, company stated no change in reported figures

Five risks, one question: which one would you weight most? 🤔

Leading Global Voices in Fashion and Sustainability Join the UK Jury for the R|Elan™ Circular Design Challenge in partnership with the UN in India 2026

The UK jury confers RWRK STUDIO by Farouk Braimoh as the finalist of the UK edition of the R|Elan CDC

United Kingdom, July 13, 2026: The R|Elan™ Circular Design Challenge (CDC), India's leading platform championing circular innovation in fashion and textiles, successfully concluded its United Kingdom Jury in London, bringing together globally respected leaders from fashion, sustainability, design and innovation to evaluate this year's cohort of pioneering circular fashion entrepreneurs. The jury reflected the growing global momentum towards accelerating circularity and responsible innovation across the fashion ecosystem, with its collective expertise playing a pivotal role in identifying solutions capable of reshaping the future of sustainable fashion.

The UK Jury comprised an exceptional line-up of industry leaders, including Matthew Needham, Creative Director of Matthew Needham and Senior Lecturer at Central Saint Martins; Shailja Dubé, Institute of Positive Fashion, British Council; Muchaneta Ten Napel, Founder and CEO of Shape Innovate; Sevra Davis, Director of Architecture, Design and Fashion at the British Council; and Radhika Kaul Batra, Chief of Staff at the United Nations Resident Coordinator's Office, India. Together, the jury represented a distinguished cross-section of experts from academia, sustainability, innovation, design and international development, driving meaningful transformation across the global fashion ecosystem.

This year's UK semi-finalists showcased a diverse range of design solutions that reimagined fashion through the lens of circularity, material innovation and responsible production. Dorota Bojanowska, founder of Dorota Bojanowska, uses screen printing as a tool to give a new life to vintage textiles and garments. Tyler Greenston, founder of Tyler Von Greenston, presented a collection rooted in pagan symbolism and humanity's relationship with nature, combining zero-waste design principles with sculptural craftsmanship and material-respect philosophies. Farouk Braimoh, founder of RWRK STUDIO, showcased his commitment to rethinking textile waste from other industries into contemporary fashion through circular design, while Heather Martin, founder of mono studio, demonstrated a thoughtful approach to responsible design through modular pattern making & production choices. Porscha Hill, founder of Bad Habits, reimagined fashion waste through garment reconstruction, natural dyeing and low-impact production, transforming pre-existing garments and textile waste into genderless collections that celebrate craftsmanship, longevity and circular design.

Following the jury's comprehensive evaluation of this year's UK semi-finalists, the distinguished UK Jury selected RWRK STUDIO by Farouk Braimoh as the United Kingdom Finalist of the eighth edition of the R|Elan™ Circular Design Challenge in partnership with the UN in India & Lakme Fashion Week.
As the Circular Design Challenge continues to expand its international footprint, the London Jury marks another significant milestone in fostering global collaboration between innovators, industry experts and sustainability advocates. By convening influential voices from across fashion, design and innovation, the platform continues to identify and elevate breakthrough ideas that champion circular business models, responsible production practices and scalable solutions for a more resilient future. The jury's diverse perspectives ensure a holistic evaluation of innovations that balance creativity, commercial viability and measurable environmental impact.

𝗜𝗻𝗱𝗶𝗮'𝘀 𝗥𝗲𝗻𝗲𝘄𝗮𝗯𝗹𝗲 𝗘𝗻𝗲𝗿𝗴𝘆 𝗥𝗮𝗰𝗲 𝗝𝘂𝘀𝘁 𝗚𝗼𝘁 𝗕𝗶𝗴𝗴𝗲𝗿.

𝗔𝗱𝗶𝘁𝘆𝗮 𝗕𝗶𝗿𝗹𝗮 𝗥𝗲𝗻𝗲𝘄𝗮𝗯𝗹𝗲𝘀 has acquired 𝗦𝗵𝗲𝗹𝗹'𝘀 𝗦𝗽𝗿𝗻𝗴 𝗘𝗻𝗲𝗿𝗴𝘆 in a ₹𝟭𝟳,𝟮𝟬𝟬 𝗰𝗿𝗼𝗿𝗲 ($𝟭.𝟴 𝗯𝗶𝗹𝗹𝗶𝗼𝗻) deal—one of the largest renewable energy acquisitions in India.

The acquisition adds approximately 𝟱 𝗚𝗪 of renewable capacity, taking Aditya Birla Renewables' portfolio to around 𝟵.𝟯 𝗚𝗪.

This isn't just another acquisition.

It's a sign that India's clean energy sector is entering a new phase where scale is being built through strategic acquisitions, not just new project development.

As electricity demand rises from manufacturing, data centres, AI infrastructure, and rapid urbanisation, owning large renewable portfolios is becoming a long-term competitive advantage.

The race is no longer just about building the next solar or wind project.

It's about building integrated energy platforms capable of powering India's next decade of economic growth.

India's renewable story is shifting from capacity creation to market consolidation—and this deal could be remembered as one of its defining milestones.

Do you think the next wave of India's energy transition will be driven more by billion-dollar acquisitions or by new greenfield renewable projects?

Every monsoon, cities spend crores trying to push rainwater away.

Rotterdam asked a different question:

It all started with an idea people once laughed at.

This isn't just a story about green roofs.
It's a masterclass in how rethinking unused spaces can make cities more climate-resilient.

Could India's rooftops become the next solution to urban flooding?

Indian Railways, established in 1853, is a critical infrastructure of India, deeply embedded in the country’s social, economic, and cultural fabric. Here's a more detailed breakdown:- 🚆 🚆 

🟠 1. **Historical Background**
   - **Foundation**: The first train ran from Mumbai to Thane in 1853.
   - **Colonial Legacy**: The British set up the railway network to facilitate movement of goods, especially raw materials, during colonial rule.
   - **Post-Independence**: After 1947, the Indian government nationalized railways, and it has since been central to national integration and economic development.

🔴2. **Infrastructure**
   - **Network**: Over 68,000 kilometers of track, with 7,300 stations across the country.
   - **Electrification**: Around 80% of the network is electrified, contributing to energy efficiency.
   - **Gauge**: Indian Railways primarily uses broad gauge (1,676 mm), though narrow and meter gauges still exist in some areas.
   - **Bridges and Tunnels**: It spans numerous geographical challenges, including the world's highest rail bridge over the Chenab River in Jammu and Kashmir.

🟡 3. **Divisions and Zones**
   - Indian Railways is divided into 18 zones, each managed by a zone-specific headquarters, such as:
     - Northern Railway (New Delhi)
     - Central Railway (Mumbai)
     - Eastern Railway (Kolkata)
     - Southern Railway (Chennai)
     - And others like Western, North Western, South Central, and East Central Railways.

⚪ 4. **Types of Trains**
   - **Passenger Trains**:
     - **Superfast Express Trains**: Rajdhani, Shatabdi, Duronto for fast intercity travel.
     - **Luxury Trains**: Palace on Wheels, Maharaja Express (high-end tourism).
     - **Suburban Trains**: For local, daily commuters in cities like Mumbai, Chennai, and Kolkata.
     - **Freight Trains**: Indian Railways is a key mover of bulk goods like coal, steel, and agricultural produce.
   
   - **New Initiatives**:
     - **Vande Bharat Express**: A semi-high-speed train, a flagship of modern Indian Railways.
     - **Dedicated Freight Corridors (DFC)**: Aimed to boost freight capacity and speed, reducing congestion on passenger routes.

⚫5. **Technology and Modernization**
   - **High-Speed Rail**: Work is ongoing on the Mumbai-Ahmedabad high-speed rail project, which will introduce bullet trains to India.
   - **Smart Stations**: Indian Railways is upgrading major stations to world-class standards, with modern amenities like Wi-Fi, digital ticketing, and green initiatives.
   - **E-ticketing**: Indian Railway Catering and Tourism Corporation (IRCTC) manages online ticket booking, catering, and tourism services.
   - **Automation**: Introduction of smart coaches with sensors, real-time monitoring, and GPS tracking for improved safety and efficiency.

🟣 6. **Economic Impact**
   - **Freight Earnings**: A significant revenue generator, transporting heavy industries like coal, iron ore, and fertilizers.
   - **Employment**: It directly employs over 1.3 million people, making it one of the world’s largest employers.
   - **Tourism**: Special trains and circuits boost tourism, offering themed experiences like hill trains (e.g., Darjeeling Himalayan Railway, a UNESCO World Heritage Site).
   - **Revenue**: Passenger traffic and freight contribute to revenue, with freight being the larger share.

🟢7. **Challenges**
   - **Overcrowding**: Despite improvements, the network is overcrowded, with passenger demand exceeding capacity.
   - **Maintenance**: Upgrading and maintaining aging infrastructure is a significant challenge.
   - **Safety**: While technology has improved, accidents due to derailments and human errors remain concerns.

Indian Railways is a massive system with ongoing developments that aim to balance affordability with modernization and sustainability. It serves as the backbone of the country’s transportation, supporting both the movement of goods and people across its vast expanse.⚡⚡

Most revolutions begin begin with people saying, "That will never work."

That's exactly what Jasmit Singh Arora heard when he started collecting discarded mango gutlis. People called him "Pagal Sardar." He ignored the laughter because he had seen something others hadn't—farmers working tirelessly yet earning too little.

Instead of letting those seeds rot, he turned them into hope. He grafted them, grew saplings, and gave them away for free, believing they could create a greener India and a better future for farmers.

Years later, that belief has become a movement. More than 21 lakh mango seeds have reached him from across the world, helping grow 8 lakh+ fruit trees and support 5,000+ farmers with long-term livelihoods.

All because one man refused to believe a mango gutli was just waste.

This mango season, don't throw the seed away. Wash it. Dry it. Send it. The smallest act you do today could become someone else's future tomorrow.
Reach him on Instagram : @mango.gutliman

#MangoRevolution #FarmersFirst #GreenIndia #ClimateAction #Sustainability

[Gutli Man, Mango Seed Movement, Sustainable Farming, Tree Plantation]

In the dry sands of Rajasthan, Sundaram Varma grew a 60,000-tree forest using just one litre of water. 🌱

For 30+ years, he’s trapped rain underground, teaching roots to find water and survive on their own.

Saving nearly 200 million litres and preserving 700 native seed varieties, his work proves one thing: India doesn’t need more water. It needs more wisdom.

Leading Global Voices in Fashion and Sustainability Join the UK Jury for the R|Elan™ Circular Design Challenge in partnership with the UN in India 2026

The UK jury confers RWRK STUDIO by Farouk Braimoh as the finalist of the UK edition of the R|Elan CDC

United Kingdom, July 13, 2026: The R|Elan™ Circular Design Challenge (CDC), India's leading platform championing circular innovation in fashion and textiles, successfully concluded its United Kingdom Jury in London, bringing together globally respected leaders from fashion, sustainability, design and innovation to evaluate this year's cohort of pioneering circular fashion entrepreneurs. The jury reflected the growing global momentum towards accelerating circularity and responsible innovation across the fashion ecosystem, with its collective expertise playing a pivotal role in identifying solutions capable of reshaping the future of sustainable fashion.

The UK Jury comprised an exceptional line-up of industry leaders, including Matthew Needham, Creative Director of Matthew Needham and Senior Lecturer at Central Saint Martins; Shailja Dubé, Institute of Positive Fashion, British Council; Muchaneta Ten Napel, Founder and CEO of Shape Innovate; Sevra Davis, Director of Architecture, Design and Fashion at the British Council; and Radhika Kaul Batra, Chief of Staff at the United Nations Resident Coordinator's Office, India. Together, the jury represented a distinguished cross-section of experts from academia, sustainability, innovation, design and international development, driving meaningful transformation across the global fashion ecosystem.

This year's UK semi-finalists showcased a diverse range of design solutions that reimagined fashion through the lens of circularity, material innovation and responsible production. Dorota Bojanowska, founder of Dorota Bojanowska, uses screen printing as a tool to give a new life to vintage textiles and garments. Tyler Greenston, founder of Tyler Von Greenston, presented a collection rooted in pagan symbolism and humanity's relationship with nature, combining zero-waste design principles with sculptural craftsmanship and material-respect philosophies. Farouk Braimoh, founder of RWRK STUDIO, showcased his commitment to rethinking textile waste from other industries into contemporary fashion through circular design, while Heather Martin, founder of mono studio, demonstrated a thoughtful approach to responsible design through modular pattern making & production choices. Porscha Hill, founder of Bad Habits, reimagined fashion waste through garment reconstruction, natural dyeing and low-impact production, transforming pre-existing garments and textile waste into genderless collections that celebrate craftsmanship, longevity and circular design.

Following the jury's comprehensive evaluation of this year's UK semi-finalists, the distinguished UK Jury selected RWRK STUDIO by Farouk Braimoh as the United Kingdom Finalist of the eighth edition of the R|Elan™ Circular Design Challenge in partnership with the UN in India & Lakme Fashion Week.
As the Circular Design Challenge continues to expand its international footprint, the London Jury marks another significant milestone in fostering global collaboration between innovators, industry experts and sustainability advocates. By convening influential voices from across fashion, design and innovation, the platform continues to identify and elevate breakthrough ideas that champion circular business models, responsible production practices and scalable solutions for a more resilient future. The jury's diverse perspectives ensure a holistic evaluation of innovations that balance creativity, commercial viability and measurable environmental impact.

Farouk Braimoh, founder of RWRK STUDIO said, “We're absolutely thrilled to be moving on to the global finals of the Circular Design Challenge. Winning the UK Jury Round is an incredible honour, especially considering the exceptional level of talent and innovation we witnessed throughout the competition. This recognition is a meaningful validation of RWRK Studio's vision and our commitment to circular design. We look forward to bringing our work to India, showcasing our collection on the global stage, and connecting with an inspiring community of designers who are redefining the future of fashion. We're deeply grateful to the jury and the organisers for their support, thoughtful feedback, and belief in our work. We're excited for what's ahead.”

Mr. Rakesh Bali, Sr. Vice President – PetChem, Reliance Industries Limited said "Congratulations to RWRK STUDIO by Farouk Braimoh on being named the UK finalist. At Reliance, we believe that the boldest voices will write fashion's next chapter. The R|Elan™ Circular Design Challenge, now in its eighth season, stands as our enduring promise to that philosophy—offering young designers the rare combination of mentorship, visibility, and the material innovation to turn circular ideals into tangible craft. As we bring this movement to the global stage through the India–France Year of Innovation 2026, we are honoring two great design traditions while demonstrating that creativity, united with sustainable purpose, can move markets and mindsets. This is India's idea of circular fashion, at last echoing across continents."

Jaspreet Chandok, Group Vice President, Reliance Brands Limited, said "Congratulations to this year's United Kingdom Finalist RWRK STUDIO by Farouk Braimoh and all the exceptional designers who presented such inspiring circular innovations. The UK Jury showcased the incredible depth of creativity and entrepreneurial thinking emerging from one of the world's leading fashion markets. As the R|Elan™ Circular Design Challenge continues its global journey, it is inspiring to see innovators from across geographies come together with a shared commitment to redefining the future of fashion through responsible design, material innovation and scalable circular solutions. We look forward to welcoming the UK Finalist to India for the Grand Jury and celebrating the next chapter of this global movement."

Sevra Davis, Director of Architecture, Design and Fashion, British Council said, “The British Council Architecture Design and Fashion team is honoured to be the U.K. partner supporting the Circular Design Challenge in partnership with the United Nations India. Over the last four years of our partnership, I have seen the programme grow in impact and influence, championing sustainable practice in the fashion and textile industry and making real change. The U.K. finalists this year demonstrate exciting perspectives, thinking and innovation in the future of  circular design, positive social and environmental impact as well as scalability. 

Following the successful conclusion of the United Kingdom Jury, the R|Elan™ Circular Design Challenge 2026 will continue its global journey with the Asia Pacific Jury on 24 July and the India Jury on 28 July. The regional finalists will then advance to the Grand Jury at the United Nations Headquarters in New Delhi before presenting their work at the finale showcase at the French Embassy in India during Lakmé Fashion Week x FDCI in October, where the Winner and Runner-Up will be announced.

With each regional milestone, the R|Elan™ Circular Design Challenge continues to strengthen its vision of building a global community of design innovators committed to redefining the future of fashion through circularity.

About R|Elan™ Circular Design Challenge (RCDC)

Launched in India in 2018, the R|Elan™ Circular Design Challenge (RCDC) is a globally recognised platform dedicated to discovering, nurturing and accelerating the next generation of entrepreneurs, designers and innovators shaping the future of circular fashion. Developed in partnership with Reliance Industries Limited’s R|Elan™, the United Nations in India and Lakmé Fashion Week, RCDC champions solutions that reduce waste and advance circularity across the fashion and textile industry. Through mentorship, industry access and global exposure, the programme supports emerging talent while fostering innovation, collaboration and systems change. RCDC continues to expand its international footprint across India, Europe, the United Kingdom, Asia Pacific and the wider global fashion ecosystem.

About R|Elan™: 
R|Elan™ is the next-gen fabric that has been created from specially engineered fibers and yarns using cutting-edge technological expertise, state-of-the-art R&D, and the robust testing systems of Reliance Industries. These smart fabrics have been designed to enhance comfort and aesthetics and it is being increasingly sought-after fabric by renowned domestic and global brands, retailers, and apparel manufacturers. The technologies that power R|Elan™ fabric imparts several attributes such as moisture management, odor-free, and thermal insulation while ensuring that they contribute something back to Mother Earth.

About United Nations in India
The United Nations system in India includes 26 organizations that serve in India. The UN entities, under the overall leadership of the UN Resident Coordinator, provide strategic support to India to help the country achieve its aspirations to end poverty and inequality and to promote sustainable development, in line with the globally agreed Sustainable Development Goal and India’s ambitious vision of Viksit Bharat 2047. UN India supports the Government and people of India in advancing the country’s ambitious commitments to rapid change and development priorities, including its leadership to enhance resource efficiency across sectors, transition to a circular economy and promotion of sustainable lifestyles.

For more information, visit: https://t.co/1O1IGfKnBU

About Reliance Industries Limited 
Reliance Industries Limited (RIL) is India’s largest private sector company, with a consolidated revenue of INR 11,75,919 crore (US$ 124.0 billion), cash profit of INR 1,71,258 crore (US$ 18.1 billion) and net profit of INR 95,754 crore (US$ 10.1 billion) for the year ended March 31, 2026. Reliance’s activities span hydrocarbon exploration and production, petroleum refining and marketing, petrochemicals, advanced materials and composites, renewables (solar and hydrogen), retail, digital services and media and entertainment.

Currently ranked 88th, Reliance is the largest private sector company from India to be featured in Fortune’s Global 500 list of 'World’s Largest Companies' for 2025. The company stands 45th in the Forbes Global 2000 rankings of 'World’s Largest Public Companies' for 2025, the highest among Indian companies. The plantation drive was followed by a Signature Campaign.

Addressing the gathering, the CMD, OIL, highlighted the company's significant contribution towards the Government of India's vision of a cleaner and greener nation. ♻️ INDIA'S RECYCLING ECONOMY | A Multi-Decade Investment Theme? 🇮🇳

India is moving rapidly towards a circular economy, where waste is no longer discarded but converted into valuable raw materials. Rising commodity prices, stricter environmental regulations, urbanisation, EV adoption, and government initiatives are creating long-term opportunities across the recycling ecosystem.

Instead of viewing recycling as a traditional business, investors are increasingly tracking it as an emerging structural growth theme.

🔋 Lead Recycling

🔹 Gravita India – One of India's largest lead recyclers with a growing presence in aluminium, plastic and rubber recycling.

🔹 Pondy Oxides & Chemicals – Major player in lead recycling and lead alloy manufacturing.

🔹 Nile Ltd – Lead recycling and battery material business catering to industrial demand.

---

♻️ Metal Recycling

🔹 Nupur Recyclers – Processes ferrous and non-ferrous scrap for industrial applications.

🔹 Jain Resource Recycling – Focused on recovering valuable metals from industrial waste.

🔹 Baheti Recycling Industries – Aluminium recycling benefiting from increasing demand for lightweight metals.

---

🗑️ Waste Management & Municipal Services

🔹 MSTC – Government-backed e-auction platform facilitating scrap disposal and resource recovery.

🔹 Antony Waste Handling Cell – Integrated municipal solid waste (MSW) collection, processing and landfill management.

🔹 A2Z Infra Engineering – Waste collection, transportation and municipal waste processing.

🔹 Urban Enviro Waste Management – Solid waste management services across multiple cities.

---

💧 Water Recycling & Sewage Treatment

🔹 VA Tech Wabag – Global leader in water treatment, desalination and wastewater recycling.

🔹 EMS Ltd – Sewage treatment plants (STPs), water supply and wastewater infrastructure projects.

🔹 Felix Industries – Industrial wastewater treatment and water recycling solutions.

---

🛞 Tyre & Rubber Recycling

🔹 GRP Ltd – Recycled rubber products with domestic and export markets.

🔹 Elgi Rubber Company – Tyre retreading and rubber recycling solutions.

🔹 Tinna Rubber & Infrastructure – Converts waste tyres into crumb rubber and modified bitumen.

---

🧴 Plastic & PET Recycling

🔹 Ganesha Ecosphere – India's leading PET bottle recycling company producing recycled polyester fibre.

🔹 Race Eco Chain – Plastic waste collection and recycling with a focus on sustainability.

---

💻 E-Waste Recycling

🔹 Cerebra Integrated Technologies – IT asset management and electronic waste recycling.

🔹 Eco Recycling (Ecoreco) – One of India's oldest organised e-waste recyclers.

🔹 Namo eWaste Management – End-to-end e-waste collection, processing and recycling.

📈 Why This Theme Could Grow Over the Next Decade

✅ Growing demand for recycled metals and plastics
✅ Rising battery recycling opportunity from EV adoption

The ICSSR–CAG Colloquium held on 13 July 2026 at the R.A. Poddar Auditorium, Institute of Management, University of Rajasthan, Jaipur, on the theme "Using CAG Data for Social Science Research and Strengthening Solid Waste Management Governance," brought together academicians, researchers, students, public administrators, and audit professionals to deliberate on the role of authentic government data in evidence-based policymaking and sustainable urban governance. The programme underscored the importance of collaboration between the Indian Council of Social Science Research (ICSSR), the Comptroller and Auditor General (CAG), universities, and the research community in advancing social science research and informed public discourse.

The programme commenced with a welcome address by Shri Ramavatar Sharma, who welcomed the participants and emphasized the growing importance of authentic government data in governance and policymaking. He encouraged students to utilise government reports and datasets for research and highlighted the significance of CAG reports in strengthening public finance and accountability.

Prof. Alpana Kateja highlighted data as a powerful tool for governance and public policy, emphasizing accessibility, democratization, and informed interpretation. Ms. Mani K. introduced ICSSR's fellowships, grants, and research opportunities, encouraging scholars to undertake evidence-based and policy-oriented studies. Shri Ravindra Yadavexplained the constitutional mandate of the CAG and introduced key publications, including the Annual Accounts, State Finance Audit Reports, Public Finance Statistics, and Fiscal Health Indicators, demonstrating how these datasets support trend analysis, inter-state comparisons, and public policy research. Prof. Ram Singh emphasized stronger collaboration between academia and public institutions and encouraged interdisciplinary research using authentic public finance data to promote transparency, accountability, and sustainable development.

The discussions on public finance and governance examined public finance as an instrument of public welfare, fiscal discipline, revenue and expenditure management, fiscal deficits, debt sustainability, financial accountability, and evidence-based policymaking. Deliberations on urban governance and municipal finance highlighted the need to strengthen the financial capacity of Urban Local Bodies, municipal finance reforms, and improved urban service delivery while addressing challenges relating to waste management, water quality, air pollution, public health, and urban infrastructure.

A dedicated session on Solid Waste Management (SWM) examined India's evolving waste management framework and audit findings, focusing on source segregation, scientific collection, treatment, recycling and disposal, decentralized composting, biomethanation, Material Recovery Facilities (MRFs), and the management of e-waste and legacy waste. The discussions also examined the Proposed Solid Waste Management Rules, 2026, highlighting reforms such as the Polluter Pays Principle, digital waste tracking, mandatory Refuse Derived Fuel (RDF), stricter landfill regulations, scientific remediation of legacy waste, and global best practices including Extended Producer Responsibility (EPR) and circular economy approaches.

The programme concluded with recommendations to strengthen solid waste management through universal source segregation, scientific waste processing, digitization of waste management systems, stronger monitoring and accountability, enhanced citizen participation, public awareness, and the promotion of the principles of Reduce, Reuse, and Recycle (3Rs). The discussions reaffirmed that authentic government data and public audit findings are invaluable resources for strengthening social science research, evidence-based policymaking, and accountable governance.

During the Session on 'Global Biogas Outlook: India, Italy, Ukraine' at the World Biogas Expo & Summit 2026 in Birmingham, UK, Dr. @ShahMeenesh , Chairman, NDDB presented India's dairy-led circular economy model. He highlighted the immense potential of the country's dairy cooperative network to scale biogas, compressed biogas (CBG) and organic fertiliser production, creating new income opportunities for farmers while strengthening energy security and contributing to global climate action.

The presentation showcased India's unique strengths as the world's largest milk producer with over 80 million rural dairy households, 302 million bovines and an estimated 1,653 million tonnes of cattle dung generated annually. Backed by a cooperative network of 22 state federations, 241 district milk unions over 2.3 lakh village dairy cooperative societies and 17 million dairy farmers, the sector provides a strong foundation for scaling sustainable biogas initiatives. Dr. Shah emphasised that dairy cooperatives ensure market access, equitable value distribution and sustainable rural development.

He outlined NDDB's scalable manure management models ranging from household biogas plants providing clean cooking fuel, organic slurry and carbon credit benefits to centralised biogas and CBG plants that generate clean energy, organic fertilisers and additional income for farmers through dung procurement. Community biogas systems were also highlighted as an effective solution for promoting clean energy and organic farming at the village level.

Dr. Shah also highlighted the India–Japan CBG Initiative, which aims to establish 1,000 CBG and organic fertiliser plants through the country's cooperative ecosystem. He further outlined NDDB's strategic partnerships with Suzuki Motor Corporation through NDDB Mrida Ltd., @SustainPlusSDG7 and the Multi-State Manure Cooperative to accelerate biogas adoption and strengthen the manure value chain across India.

Concluding his address, Dr. Shah reiterated that manure is a valuable resource, not waste and reaffirmed NDDB's commitment to advancing the circular bioeconomy through global partnerships. He noted that Indian dairy cooperative model offers a scalable pathway for renewable energy, climate resilience, farmer prosperity and inclusive rural development.

♻️📘 Hon'ble Chairman, NDMC Shri Keshav Chandra, IAS, launched the 'Waste Wise Citizens' Handbook and flagged off RRR on Wheels 🚛 in the presence of senior NDMC officials, representatives of Chintan Environmental Research and Action Group, RWAs, MTAs, educational institutions, BWGs, sanitation officials and other stakeholders.

💬 Shri Keshav Chandra stated that segregation at source is the foundation of scientific waste management and that its success depends on the active participation of every citizen alongside NDMC. He urged residents to adopt the principles of Reduce, Reuse and Recycle to build a cleaner and more sustainable New Delhi.

✨ Key Highlights:
🔹 Launched the 'Waste Wise Citizens' Handbook' to promote awareness and implementation of the Solid Waste Management Rules, 2026.
🔹 Flagged off RRR on Wheels for doorstep collection of segregated recyclable waste.
🔹 Promotes 4-stream waste segregation – 🌱 Wet | 📦 Dry | 🩺 Sanitary | ⚠️ Special Care Waste.
🔹 Strengthens the Reduce • Reuse • Recycle (RRR) model and supports a circular economy.

On the sidelines of the World Biogas Expo & Summit 2026 in Birmingham, UK, Dr. @ShahMeenesh , Chairman, NDDB met Ms. Charlotte Morton OBE, Chief Executive, World Biogas Association (WBA) to review the progress of the ongoing collaboration under the existing MoU between NDDB and the @WBAtweets .

During the meeting, Ms. Morton requested NDDB to become the Country Founding Member of the 'Making Biogas Happen' (MBH) India Programme. She also extended an invitation to Chairman, NDDB to deliver the Keynote Address at the WBA India Congress 2026, scheduled to be held in New Delhi in October 2026.

Dr. Shah apprised Ms. Morton of India's recent advancements in the biogas sector in India including the India–Japan CBG Initiative launched during the India–Japan Joint Economic Forum. He also highlighted NDDB's scalable manure management models that transform cattle dung into clean energy, organic fertilisers and carbon credits, creating additional income opportunities for farmers while advancing renewable energy, climate resilience and the circular bioeconomy. He also invited Ms. Morton to visit NDDB's manure management models in India to experience the cooperative-led circular economy initiatives firsthand.

NATRAX – National Automotive Test Tracks, a division of NAB and an autonomous body under the Ministry of Heavy Industries, signed a Memorandum of Understanding (MoU) with NAMTECH – New Age Makers Institute of Technology, an initiative of ArcelorMittal Nippon Steel India.

The partnership aims to strengthen India’s automotive and advanced manufacturing ecosystem by integrating NATRAX’s world-class automotive testing, certification and R&D infrastructure with NAMTECH’s expertise in advanced engineering education and skill development.

The collaboration will focus on:
* Electric Mobility, Hydrogen Mobility, Alternate Fuels and Smart Automotive Manufacturing
* Industry-aligned curricula and advanced skilling programmes
* Student internships, live projects and experiential learning
* Startup mentoring, hackathons and innovation initiatives
* Sustainability, green mobility and circular economy
* Joint conferences, workshops and knowledge-sharing platforms

This collaboration reinforces the shared commitment of NATRAX and NAMTECH to bridge the industry-academia gap, foster innovation, and develop a future-ready workforce in support of the vision of Viksit Bharat and Aatmanirbhar Bharat.

One investing hack is to pay attention to where some of the top global investors are putting money

Temasek  is a Singapore state‑owned investment company that acts like a global, long‑term equity owner and capital allocator across listed and private assets, with a record net portfolio, diversified sector exposure and a strategy built around structural trends rather than short‑term trading. Temasek positions itself as an “investor, institution and steward,” taking significant ownership stakes in companies and seeking to influence strategy and governance over long horizons. As of March 31, 2026, its net portfolio value was S$518 billion, up from S$434 billion at March 31, 2025.
Temasek plans to keep investing heavily along its four structural trends, including AI and digital infrastructure, sustainable living (green energy, circular economy), new consumption models and healthcare/aging. New initiatives include building a S$10 billion private credit platform, deeper forays into AI infrastructure through partnerships with players like Microsoft and BlackRock, and continued expansion of its asset‑management arms (Seviora, 65 Equity Partners, Vertex). The biggest strategic emphasis is on three growth themes: AI, core-plus infrastructure, and private credit. Temasek plans to expand AI exposure to up to 15% of portfolio value by 2031, core-plus infrastructure to 5%, and private credit to 5%.
Dilhan Pillay’s tone was constructive but cautious: the external environment was described as a “polycrisis” with geopolitics, energy security, inflation, and AI disruption all colliding at once. The core management response is to build a more resilient portfolio that can absorb shocks, preserve liquidity, and keep compounding over long periods rather than optimizing for any single year. He also emphasized that Temasek is not trying to chase the market, but to underwrite quality businesses, protect downside, and selectively increase exposure where structural trends are strongest. He describes investing “across the AI value chain” (five areas, some vertically integrated, creating a flywheel) and emphasizes that 85% of the portfolio value creation must come from AI adoption in non-AI names, not just in AI innovators themselves. Pillay outlines four AI pillars: “AI-Enabling Ourselves” (digital fluency, AI agents, job redesign), “AI-Proofing Our Portfolio” (future-proofing TPCs and managing AI risks), “AI-Scaling Our Portfolio” (selective scaling across the AI value chain), and “AI-Diffusion” (AI immersion programmes for TPC leaders in Silicon Valley, Shanghai, and Hangzhou).
Temasek continued reallocating toward larger, higher-conviction opportunities and away from some legacy exposures, including a planned exit from Schneider Electric India and new investments such as Anthropic, OpenAI, ANE, and Luckin Coffee. Management said the portfolio remains about half listed and half unlisted, and the refreshed organization is meant to sharpen strategy across Singapore portfolio companies, global direct investments, and partnerships/funds. The firm also noted stronger activity in capital recycling and value-creation work, especially in larger portfolio companies and asset-management platforms.
