Solid Waste Management Rules, 2026 to come into effect from April 1

Ministry of Environment, Forest and Climate Change, Government of India notified the Solid Waste Management Rules, 2026 on 27th January 2026 in the Official Gazette, superseding the Solid Waste Management Rules, 2016. The rules shall come into effect from April 1, 2026. The revised rules integrate the principles of circular economy and extended producer responsibility, with a specific focus on efficient waste segregation and management.

The Solid Waste Management Rules, 2026, mandate four-stream segregation of solid waste at source into wet waste, dry waste, sanitary waste and special care waste and prescribe clear definition of bulk waste generators who have a mandate to fulfil Extended Bulk Waste Generator Responsibility, in order to ensure that the waste generated is collected, transported and processed in an environmentally sound manner. The rules also operationalize the online tracking and monitoring of all stages of solid waste management through Centralised Online Portal including collection, transportation, processing and disposal.

The rules prescribe specific roles and responsibilities for urban and rural local bodies, State/ UT Governments and concerned Ministries at Government of India. The rules prescribe an increase in the fuel substitution rate from current 5 % to 15 % over a six year period for industrial units, including cement plants and waste-to-energy plants for use of Refuse Derived Fuel (RDF).

Upcoming Symposium:  Bringing informal waste pickers into the waste value chain

The informal waste workforce plays a critical, yet often invisible, role in managing India’s waste.  While the Solid Waste Management Rules, 2016, mandate their integration, on-ground  progress has been apathetic, with only limited efforts towards meaningful inclusion.

Next week, the Centre for Science and Environment (CSE) is hosting a vital national symposium to discuss the current landscape of informal waste-picking sector in India and policy reforms needed to foster meaningful inclusion.

We will also be releasing a new report '“Pathways to Inclusion of Waste Pickers' that explores real-world models across India, their viability, and the nuanced debate between integration vs formalisation.

🗓️ Date: August 6, 2025
📍 Location: India Habitat Centre (Magnolia Hall), Delhi

The ICSSR–CAG Colloquium held on 13 July 2026 at the R.A. Poddar Auditorium, Institute of Management, University of Rajasthan, Jaipur, on the theme "Using CAG Data for Social Science Research and Strengthening Solid Waste Management Governance," brought together academicians, researchers, students, public administrators, and audit professionals to deliberate on the role of authentic government data in evidence-based policymaking and sustainable urban governance. The programme underscored the importance of collaboration between the Indian Council of Social Science Research (ICSSR), the Comptroller and Auditor General (CAG), universities, and the research community in advancing social science research and informed public discourse.

The programme commenced with a welcome address by Shri Ramavatar Sharma, who welcomed the participants and emphasized the growing importance of authentic government data in governance and policymaking. He encouraged students to utilise government reports and datasets for research and highlighted the significance of CAG reports in strengthening public finance and accountability.

Prof. Alpana Kateja highlighted data as a powerful tool for governance and public policy, emphasizing accessibility, democratization, and informed interpretation. Ms. Mani K. introduced ICSSR's fellowships, grants, and research opportunities, encouraging scholars to undertake evidence-based and policy-oriented studies. Shri Ravindra Yadavexplained the constitutional mandate of the CAG and introduced key publications, including the Annual Accounts, State Finance Audit Reports, Public Finance Statistics, and Fiscal Health Indicators, demonstrating how these datasets support trend analysis, inter-state comparisons, and public policy research. Prof. Ram Singh emphasized stronger collaboration between academia and public institutions and encouraged interdisciplinary research using authentic public finance data to promote transparency, accountability, and sustainable development.

The discussions on public finance and governance examined public finance as an instrument of public welfare, fiscal discipline, revenue and expenditure management, fiscal deficits, debt sustainability, financial accountability, and evidence-based policymaking. Deliberations on urban governance and municipal finance highlighted the need to strengthen the financial capacity of Urban Local Bodies, municipal finance reforms, and improved urban service delivery while addressing challenges relating to waste management, water quality, air pollution, public health, and urban infrastructure.

A dedicated session on Solid Waste Management (SWM) examined India's evolving waste management framework and audit findings, focusing on source segregation, scientific collection, treatment, recycling and disposal, decentralized composting, biomethanation, Material Recovery Facilities (MRFs), and the management of e-waste and legacy waste. The discussions also examined the Proposed Solid Waste Management Rules, 2026, highlighting reforms such as the Polluter Pays Principle, digital waste tracking, mandatory Refuse Derived Fuel (RDF), stricter landfill regulations, scientific remediation of legacy waste, and global best practices including Extended Producer Responsibility (EPR) and circular economy approaches.

The programme concluded with recommendations to strengthen solid waste management through universal source segregation, scientific waste processing, digitization of waste management systems, stronger monitoring and accountability, enhanced citizen participation, public awareness, and the promotion of the principles of Reduce, Reuse, and Recycle (3Rs). The discussions reaffirmed that authentic government data and public audit findings are invaluable resources for strengthening social science research, evidence-based policymaking, and accountable governance.

author.name: Indian Council of Social Science Research (ICSSR)

Nikhil Kamath has flagged water management as India’s next massive business opportunity, arguing that it could become a multi-billion dollar sector outperforming hyped tech waves like Artificial Intelligence (AI) and Electric Vehicles (EVs). 

In mid-2026, the Zerodha co-founder posted a data-driven warning highlighting a stark structural mismatch between India's economic ambitions and its vital resource limitations. 

The Core Problem: The Disconnect

Water Guzzlers: India’s four most critical economic growth bets—food production (agriculture), nuclear energy, pharmaceuticals, and AI data centres—are immensely water-intensive. 

Dry States: These booming industries are rapidly expanding in regions and states that are already facing severe water stress and depleting groundwater reserves. 

The "Free" Illusion: Currently, water in India remains largely unpriced, heavily subsidised, or completely unmetered at the point of industrial use, leading to extreme inefficiency and widespread wastage. 

The Trillion-Dollar Business Opportunity

Kamath argues that "somewhere in that gap is a company worth building," noting that the quiet repricing of water will birth the next generation of Indian unicorns. The real wealth engine will not come from packaging or selling water itself, but rather from building the infrastructure to measure, manage, and conserve it. 

The critical startup areas include:

Smart Metering & Analytics: Creating hardware and platforms to track water usage, as companies cannot optimize what they do not measure.

Industrial Wastewater Recycling: Deploying high-efficiency circular tech so factories and data centres can treat and reuse their own drainage.

Desalination Infrastructure: Advancing tech that purifies seawater at scale to reduce the burden on shrinking inland freshwater tables.

Precision Agritech: Building smart drip-irrigation systems to cut down the massive 89-90% chunk of groundwater currently swallowed up by traditional farming.

A video shared by Abhishiek Agarwal praises the efforts of a retired school principal from Himachal Pradesh who mailed hard-to-recycle household waste to Hyderabad-based recycling initiative Goodeebag. Instead of discarding empty medicine blister packs, old toothbrushes and a manual syringe/needle destroyer box, she packed them in a reused Amazon shipping box to ensure they could be recycled responsibly.

👉🏼 A Gesture That Earned Appreciation
After receiving the parcel, a Goodeebag representative thanked the woman for her thoughtful initiative and opened the package on camera, revealing neatly sorted and rubber-banded medicine blister packs. He said such efforts reflect care for nature and a desire to protect the planet for future generations.

👉🏼 A Call for Responsible Recycling
The video encourages people to collect recyclable waste and mail it to Goodeebag using the address shared in its captions and links, promoting responsible disposal of materials that are often difficult to recycle.

👉🏼 The Logical Indian's Perspective

Acts of environmental responsibility, however small, deserve recognition because they can inspire others to make more mindful choices. Could more people adopt simple recycling habits that help reduce waste in their communities?

India has quietly taken a strategic step in the critical minerals value chain by restricting the export of black mass — a material that remains largely unknown to mainstream investors but plays a crucial role in battery recycling.

In a single year, India reportedly exported black mass containing approximately 350 tonnes of cobalt, 71.7 tonnes of lithium, and 215 tonnes of nickel. These valuable minerals were not extracted from mines; they were recovered from discarded lithium-ion batteries. While India imports these critical minerals at significant cost, a large portion of their recovered value was being exported overseas.

Black mass is generated after shredding lithium-ion batteries and typically contains 40–50% of the battery’s weight in recoverable materials, including lithium, cobalt, nickel, and other strategic elements. Due to the advanced refining capabilities and economies of scale available in countries like China and South Korea, overseas buyers often offered better prices than domestic refiners. As a result, India retained the lower-value shredding activity while exporting the higher-value mineral recovery opportunity.

The recent policy changes indicate a clear shift in India’s approach — moving from simple battery waste processing toward domestic critical mineral refining.

Key policy developments:

🔹 January 2025: The MoEFCC Technical Review Committee classified black mass as hazardous waste unless refined at the same premises. Export restrictions were tightened, requiring government approval and limiting exports to authorised end users.

🔹 October 2025: The ₹1,500 crore Critical Mineral Recycling Scheme was introduced, offering a 20% capital subsidy. However, eligibility is focused on advanced chemical recyclers capable of achieving 80% recovery rates with 99% purity standards — effectively excluding businesses focused only on shredding and exporting.

🔹 April 2026: Under the scheme, 58 companies received approval, marking the beginning of a more structured domestic recycling ecosystem.

The message is clear: the policy does not aim to eliminate black mass production; it aims to discourage the low-value “shred and export” model and encourage value-added refining within India.

This creates a significant opportunity as India’s battery recycling sector remains at an early stage. Currently, only a small fraction of end-of-life lithium-ion batteries are recycled into usable materials, while NITI Aayog estimates that India could generate around 128 GWh of recyclable battery capacity by 2030.

Companies such as Gravita India, which has expanded into lithium-ion battery recycling, along with players like Tata Chemicals, Exide Industries, and Amara Raja Energy & Mobility, are positioned in different parts of the emerging ecosystem. Private players such as Lohum and Attero are also active in advanced recycling and material recovery.

India’s next opportunity may not just be in manufacturing batteries, but in controlling the entire battery lifecycle — collection, recycling, refining, and recovery of critical minerals.

Educational purpose only. This is not a buy/sell recommendation or investment advice.

@CPCB_OFFICIAL @mpcb_official @MAHA_UDD @navalMH @prithvirajbp 

In India, small plastic water bottles have transcended their utilitarian purpose to become symbols of convenience and modernity. In government meetings, from district-level offices to national assemblies, these bottles are a common sight on conference tables, often bearing branded labels and provided as a standard amenity. Their use reflects concerns about the inconsistent quality of tap water and a lack of infrastructure for reusable alternatives, even in official settings meant to model environmental responsibility.

Similarly, marriage ceremonies—central to India’s cultural fabric—have embraced small plastic bottles as a practical solution to hydrate large gatherings. Weddings, which can host hundreds or thousands of guests, rely heavily on packaged water to ensure safety and convenience. From welcome drinks to banquet tables, these bottles are handed out freely, often discarded without a second thought. A single large wedding can generate thousands of used bottles, contributing to the 40% of India’s plastic waste that remains uncollected or unrecycled, clogging drains, littering venues, and polluting nearby water bodies.

The scale of this issue is staggering. India’s packaged water industry, driven by brands like Bisleri and Kinley, has seen exponential growth, with PET bottle consumption for water projected to have reached 3.2 billion units by 2021. This proliferation is particularly pronounced in urban areas, where rapid urbanization and unreliable municipal water supply drive demand, but it is also increasingly common in rural areas, where small bottles and sachets are marketed as affordable solutions.

Environmental Toll of Convenience
The widespread use of small plastic water bottles has dire environmental consequences. Of India’s 9.3 million tonnes of annual plastic waste, an estimated one-fifth contributes to global plastic pollution, with small bottles being a significant culprit. Only about 30% of this waste is recycled, despite claims of higher rates, leaving the rest to pile up in landfills, rivers, and oceans. India’s coastal regions, including Mumbai and Kerala, rank among the world’s most plastic-polluted, with 11 metric tonnes of plastic entering the oceans annually. Microplastics from degraded bottles infiltrate the food chain, harming marine life and posing health risks to humans.

The environmental impact is particularly visible after large events like weddings, where discarded bottles litter venues and nearby areas. Open burning of uncollected plastic waste releases toxic pollutants, contributing to air pollution and exacerbating climate change, with some plastic additives having a greenhouse gas impact far greater than carbon dioxide. These consequences are not just environmental but social, disproportionately affecting marginalized communities like waste pickers who handle plastic waste under hazardous conditions for minimal income.

Government’s Response and Its Shortcomings
The Indian government has acknowledged the plastic pollution crisis and introduced measures to address it. The Plastic Waste Management (PWM) Rules of 2016, amended in 2021, aimed to phase out single-use plastics by 2022, with a nationwide ban on items like straws and thin bags enforced from July 1, 2022. Extended Producer Responsibility (EPR) guidelines were introduced to hold manufacturers accountable for recycling. Prime Minister Narendra Modi’s 2018 pledge to eliminate single-use plastics by 2022 set an ambitious tone, and states like Sikkim and Tamil Nadu have taken steps to ban plastic bottles in government offices and promote alternatives like steel containers.

Yet, the continued presence of small plastic water bottles in government meetings and public events like weddings highlights significant gaps in implementation. Several factors contribute to this failure:

Inconsistent Enforcement: The 2022 ban exempted small plastic bottles, allowing their continued use. Enforcement of existing regulations is weak, with state pollution control boards and local authorities often lacking the resources or will to monitor compliance. A CPCB app for reporting violations has seen limited uptake, reflecting low public engagement.

Lack of Alternatives: In government settings, the absence of reliable filtered water systems or reusable bottle stations perpetuates reliance on packaged water. At weddings, the high cost of alternatives like glass bottles or water dispensers deters organizers, especially for large gatherings.

Top 10 Multi-Bagger Learnings of the month till now!

1. Export of Indian Cables and Wires industry has grown at a CAGR of 16% since FY17. Recently, the major listed players announced that the industry will grow faster between FY23-FY27 than it has in the past. Earlier the industry used to grow at 1.5x the GDP. Now, its expected industry will grow 2x the GDP growth going forward.

2. India has only 1300 sewage treatment plant as against 20,000-30,000 sewage treatment plants in China. Barely 1/3rd of Waste water is treated in India. Multiple players in the Water infrastructure theme that are doing well. You can learn about the sector from here https://t.co/fUbmbD2XKr

3. China's TTM PB has been at the lowest level since 2001. 

4. India has introduced mandatory regulations for the recycling and reuse of plastic packaging starting from FY2025 to control the plastic waste menace. India is the third-largest plastic waste generator globally.

The new PWM rules aim to implement waste management in five categories of plastic packaging: Rigid, flexible, multi-layered plastic, plastic sheet/compostable plastics and biodegradable plastics ,covering both pre-consumer and post-consumer plastics. These rules establish the responsibility of producers, importers as well as brand owners (PIBOs), among other obligated entities, who use these types of plastic to recollect (EPR effective FY2022), recycle (effective FY2025) and use of recycled plastic in the new packaging products manufactured/used by them (effective FY2026), with the minimum thresholds for each becoming more stringent over the years. In addition, these rules are also applicable to plastic waste processors, manufacturers and importers of plastic raw material and manufacturers of items made from compostable plastics or biodegradable plastics. (Kotak daily)

5. The share of luxury homes sold in India has tripled over the past five years, according to a report by property consultant Anarock Group. Luxury homes — categorized as those priced over 15 million rupees ($179,650) — made up 21% of all residential units sold across the top seven Indian cities in the first quarter of 2024, vs 7% for the same period in 2019. Affordable housing units saw their share of sales decline to 18% from 37% in the same period.

Solid Waste Rules.text: @moment_mirthful @elonmusk Bro in 2025 they don't even have toilets in India.. you're posting pictures with trash bins in the 90s.....

•Early 1900s: Some cities, like Mysore, had organized street sweeping and composting systems.
•1960s and 1970s: Municipalities began providing bins for waste, but they were not always effective. Waste was often carried in open-top trucks, and most of it was dumped rather than properly treated.
•2000s: The Municipal Solid Waste Rules of 2000 created a legal framework for integrated waste management, including waste segregation at the source. 
The Swachh Bharat Mission (SBM) and bin deployment
The most significant push for systematic bin deployment came with the Swachh Bharat Mission (Clean India Mission), a nationwide campaign launched in 2014. 
•Initial focus: The first phase of the SBM primarily focused on eliminating open defecation, which included building millions of toilets across India.

quotedTweet.text: In the USA, nearly all cities enforce "pooper-scooper" laws requiring owners to bag and trash dog waste in public spaces, with fines from $50–$500. Japan mandates immediate cleanup with bags (pee often watered down), plus pre-walk bathroom breaks—strict urban etiquette with high compliance. China’s city bylaws (e.g., in Hubei and major metros) require leashing, registration, and waste removal for sanitation, with warnings/fines for violations. All three prioritize public cleanup over pads for most breeds.

Solid Waste Rules.text:
India has municipal bylaws in cities like Delhi (Rs 500 fine under solid waste rules), Bengaluru (High Court-ordered higher penalties for parks), Mumbai, and others requiring pet owners to clean up after dogs. 

Enforcement is the gap—municipal bodies lack patrols, awareness is low, and it's seen as impractical without dedicated teams. 

Stray dogs (est. 60M+) add to the mess since no owner is liable; ABC sterilization helps population but not waste, which ties back to poor garbage management feeding them. The "inhuman" pushback usually hits stray control proposals, not pet scooping laws.

Delhi HC seeks Centre’s reply on PIL against municipal solid waste rules 2016

The United States Coast Guard has awarded India a Certificate of Appreciation under the Quality Shipping for the 21st Century (QUALSHIP 21) Program.

This recognition is based on India’s outstanding Port State Control (PSC) record, with merchant vessels flagged by India consistently meeting the highest benchmarks of safety, security, and environmental compliance. Notably, India’s three-year detention rate has remained below the 1% threshold required for inclusion in the program.

This achievement reflects the exceptional commitment and professionalism of the Directorate General of Shipping, our surveyors, and all maritime stakeholders working tirelessly to uphold international standards. 🇮🇳

NITI Aayog Proposes Model Pharma Chapter for Future FTAs, Urging India to Move Up the Value Chain from ‘Volume to Value’

What Happened?
In the eighth edition of its Trade Watch Quarterly, government think tank NITI Aayog proposed a model pharmaceutical chapter to serve as a standard blueprint for India's future free trade agreements (FTAs). The proposal aims to institutionalize regulatory predictability, improve market access, and counter non-tariff trade barriers for Indian drug exporters. Simultaneously, NITI Aayog urged the domestic sector to diversify beyond traditional generic manufacturing into knowledge-intensive, high-value segments like biopharmaceuticals, biologics, and advanced therapeutics.

What it Means?
While India is globally recognized as the pharmacy of the world supplying 50% of Africa’s, 40% of the US’s, and 25% of the UK’s generic drug requirements its global export share by value remains constrained at just 2.8% to 4%. By standardizing pharma protocols within FTAs, India seeks to upgrade its global trade positioning. Transitioning to deep-tech innovation and offering high-quality, branded products will allow Indian companies to capture a larger share of the massive $1.3 trillion global pharmaceutical market.

The Catch
India's ambitions face a critical structural vulnerability: its pharmaceutical supply chain remains heavily dependent on China, sourcing over 65% of its critical active pharmaceutical ingredients (APIs), key starting materials (KSMs), and chemical intermediates from the country. Furthermore, India's R&D intensity lags at just 7% of net sales compared to the 15–20% global standard. This deficit, combined with escalating manufacturing costs due to strict environmental compliance and delayed patent approvals, continues to create long-term uncertainty for potential innovators.

The Bottomline
To graduate from being a mass producer of low-cost generics to an undisputed capital for global pharma innovation, India must aggressively fix its supply vulnerabilities and research deficits. Success hinges on scaling domestic API production, introducing time-bound patent grant processes, and strengthening industry-academia technology transfers through life sciences clusters. 

#NITIAayog #PharmaSector #FreeTradeAgreement #MakeInIndia #API #Biopharmaceuticals #GlobalTrade

Historical Wealth Building:

1. Colonize Resource-Rich Areas
- British East India Company (1757): Seized Bengal
- Belgian Congo (1885): Leopold II's personal colony

- French West Africa (1895): 8 resource colonies

- India: $45 trillion extracted (1765-1938)
- Congo: 1,000 tons ivory + 75,000 tons rubber (1885-1908)
- Indonesia: 40% of Dutch budget from colonies (1830-1870)
- Ghana: 113 million ounces gold taken (1700-1900)

3. Block Local Industry
- India: Destroyed textile manufacturing (1750-1850)
- Egypt: Banned industrial development (1882-1956)
- China: Forced deindustrialization (1839-1949)
- Ottoman: Blocked manufacturing (1838-1914)

4. Force Raw Exports
- Cotton: India forced to export raw, import finished
- Rubber: Congo forced to extract, no processing
- Minerals: South Africa exports raw, imports processed
- Oil: Middle East extraction, European refinement

Modern Development Blocking:

1. "Structural Adjustment"
- Argentina (1991): Forced privatization of 90% state firms
- Ghana (1983-2024): 84 IMF conditions on development
- Indonesia (1997): Required selling 40 state companies
- Mexico (1982): Mandated market opening during crisis

2. "Market Liberalization"
- South Korea (1997): Forced to open financial markets
- Brazil (1998): Required removal of industrial protection
- Thailand (1997): Mandated foreign ownership rights
- Nigeria (1986): Compelled to end import substitution

3. "Debt Sustainability"
- Greece (2010): Austerity cuts development spending
- Pakistan (2019): IMF blocks infrastructure projects
- Sri Lanka (2022): Debt crisis stops industrialization
- Ecuador (2019): IMF requires cuts to development

4. "Environmental Compliance"
- Congo (2024): Blocked from developing oil fields
- Indonesia (2023): Required to stop coal power
- Nigeria (2022): Forced to abandon gas projects
- India (2024): Pressured to stop industrial expansion

@Grok, could you provide me with just 1 other example where "carbon sequestration" would be a criterion for a MULTI-BILLION $ purchase of U-boats anywhere else in the world?

No other examples exist worldwide.

Submarine/U-boat purchases (Australia AUKUS, India, Norway, Europe, etc.) focus on capability, stealth, interoperability, cost, and local industry jobs. Carbon sequestration/CCS has never been a criterion or bundled offset in any other multi-billion-dollar naval deal.

This element is unique to the German TKMS bid in Canada's current procurement, where they proposed a joint carbon capture venture in Alberta alongside the $86B+ economic package. 

Standard military procurement doesn't mix in climate tech like this.

China recently awarded its first “practical PhDs” doctoral degrees evaluated on real-world products instead of traditional research papers.

It raises an important question for India.

How should PhD education evolve in a world where innovation cycles move faster than academic publishing?

India stands at a moment where research can play a transformative role across public health, agriculture, sustainability, climate resilience, digital infrastructure, and education.

Scaling research capacity is becoming increasingly important for the next phase of national innovation.

Think about the problems ahead:
-» Healthcare access for billions
-» Climate resilience
-» Agricultural productivity
-» Urban infrastructure
-» AI and deep technology

These challenges require deep research, far beyond quick solutions.

Doctoral education has historically been one of the most powerful engines of innovation. Many foundational breakthroughs across computing, medicine, and engineering have emerged from strong research ecosystems built around PhD programs.

The real opportunity for India lies in expanding PhD programs while increasing their real-world impact.

Encouraging applied research alongside theoretical work, recognizing prototypes and technologies as meaningful doctoral outcomes, reducing administrative delays, and focusing evaluation on real contribution rather than just publication counts can significantly strengthen the ecosystem.

• More researchers.
• More experimentation.
• More real-world impact.

India’s next wave of breakthroughs in healthcare, climate tech, agriculture, and AI will likely come from the work being done by PhD researchers today!

Thanks to Biju Dharmapalan Sir for the insightful article: https://t.co/ppFstJHlC1

I love supporting new founders.

A few days ago I met @InderanKannan, the founder of OnlyScrews.

His story stuck with me.

He wasn't trying to build a screw company.

He was building a climate-tech startup.

While prototyping, they could source almost every component online.

Except one - SCREWS!

They needed 10.

Suppliers wanted them to buy 200.

Finding the right size took days.

Instead of assuming it was just their problem, they spoke to founders at IIT Kanpur and SRM.

Turns out almost everyone building hardware in India had the same frustration.

So in May 2024, they ran a simple experiment.

They launched https://t.co/n9nNeVyRG1

They started with around 90 products.

Today they stock 2,000+ SKUs, with packs built for:

• 3D printing
• Drone builders
• Repairs
• R&D labs
• Small manufacturers

No minimum order.

Fast shipping across India.

They stock their own inventory.

They're not another marketplace.

Ironically...

Their original startup never found product-market fit.

This one did.

I love stories like this because the best businesses often don't start with a grand vision.

They start with someone getting frustrated enough to fix a problem they couldn't ignore.

In the US, builders have McMaster-Carr.

If @InderanKannan succeeds, India might finally have its own version.

If you're building hardware, give him a follow.

We need more founders solving problems like these.

I have to say, the energy in the Hyderabad startup ecosystem is infectious. I was surprised to meet founders that are 50+ in age but have the eagerness to build of a 20-year-old. And crazy grit, ambition for a city that doesn’t boast of as many VCs as Bangalore/Delhi. 

Let’s talk numbers on why I see the Hyderabad startup landscape grow stronger in the coming years:

— Startups from Hyd have raised more than $141 billion in the last decade

— Many status have gone to raise over $100 million in funding since 2014, with notable names including Greenko Group ($355 million), Zenoti ($331 million), Spandana Sphoorty ($251 million), Smartron ($225 million), Zenwork ($163 million), Lauras Labs ($148 million), HighRadius ($125 million), and Darwinbox ($115.9 million), among others

— 4,369 total startups founded in the city so far and counting 

— Strong government backing with institutions like T-Hub, We-Hub, and many more 

— Has the largest manufacturing and prototyping innovation hub in Asia, T-Works (happy to connect any founders in deep tech with them) 

— Focused accelerators for startups building in fintech, health tech, deep tech, biotech, climate tech, and gaming

Overall, very bullish on startup hubs becoming robust in cities beyond the Delhi-Bangalore-Mumbai circle.

EIL was represented by Shri Anurag Sinha (Executive Director, Technical) as a panellist at the “StartUp MahaKumbh (Bharat Innovates), Bharat Mandapam, April 3-5, New Delhi”. Speaking on the topic of “New Technologies Enabling Reduction in Emissions from Energy Use in Buildings” under the theme “Energy & Climate Tech”, Shri Anurag Sinha emphasised upon the recent advancements in the ecosystem towards reduction in emissions, including the upcoming revision of the National Building Code of India (NBC), which was very well appreciated by the audience.

The event, World's biggest showcase of Innovation and entrepreneurship, focused on the journey and vision of India's startup ecosystem over the next two decades, aiming to shape a self-reliant and innovative and #ViksitBharatby2047. It highlighted the evolving policies, people, and partnerships that will drive the country's transformation into a global leader in innovation and entrepreneurship, and was led by FICCI, ASSOCHAM, NASSCOM, TIE, IVCA & BF, presented by GeM, powered by SIDBI & ECGC and supported by DPIIT & MeitY, Energy & Climate Tech was fuelled by EIL along with other Oil & Gas majors.

Shri Anurag Sinha is also a member of the National Building Code of India committee, which is considered as the Bible for the Building Industry.

What if the venture capital industry's biggest blind spot isn't technology... but women founders' talent? | India Specific - Global General

Imagine an asset class that consistently delivers stronger returns, better capital efficiency, lower burn rates and greater market expansion. Now imagine that it receives just 2.3% of global venture capital funding. That asset class isn't AI. It isn't climate tech. It isn't quantum computing. It's women founders.

At what point does this stop being a conversation about gender and become a conversation about poor capital allocation? If markets are supposed to reward performance, why does the data keep pointing in one direction while the money flows in another?

I've explored this uncomfortable paradox in the latest issue of Edward Zander Review. Perhaps the real question isn't whether women founders deserve more capital. It's whether investors can afford to keep overlooking one of the market's most underpriced opportunities.

Read the article. Then ask yourself: Is this an equality gap, or an investment failure?

Climate Tech.text:
🎥 Video Recap – Punkvism visits Low Carbon

After traveling across Indonesia, Dubai, and India, the Punkvism team made a special stop in Gangjin-gun to meet with Low Carbon, a Korean startup developing cutting-edge tech to fight the climate crisis.

What we learned 👇
🌍 CO₂ levels are rising fast and we’re nearing the tipping point.
Low Carbon is tackling this with tech like DAC, CCU, and DACCUS — capturing carbon directly from the air and turning it into carbon-neutral fuel using green hydrogen.
🧪 Their materials (KLC) selectively capture CO₂ from chimneys and open air.
🚢 The captured carbon is repurposed to power vehicles, ships, and aircraft.
📚 With 400+ patents and international expansion underway, this isn’t theory — it’s happening.

And here’s where Punkvism comes in:
💠 Web3 meets climate tech.
We’re exploring Carbon Mining where captured CO₂ data is stored on-chain and tokenized.
1kg of CO₂ = recorded = rewarded.
Real-world impact becomes a real-world asset. That’s RWA done right.

💡 It’s not just about tech, it’s about building a global, values-driven community that rewards contribution, not just capital.

This is what Web3 activism looks like.
And we’re just getting started. 🌱💜

Watch the full video here:
https://t.co/6RU6Bii9Ka
